text: **The Book of Unique Events** by Li Kang of the Tang Dynasty **Preface** The *Book of Unique Events* records the extraordinary occurrences in the world. From the beginning of time to the present day, it encompasses all that can be seen and heard, including immortals, ghosts, and monsters. While there are many extensively recorded events, I have compiled them into three volumes without considering my own limitations. I hope this work will be passed on to the learned, for the sake of bringing smiles. **Volume One** Yi Yin was born without a father and emerged from a mulberry tree. Yu the Great's wife transformed into a stone and gave birth to Qi by cutting open her belly. Laozi had ears seven feet long and was born after eighty-one years in his mother's womb, emerging from her left side. At birth, he had white hair and a full beard. Xu Yanzhong, the King of Xu, had no bones but possessed great virtue. Liu Yong had a peculiar habit of eating human scabs. Wen Wang, a virtuous ruler of the Zhou dynasty, had four breasts. Gao Yao, a legendary judge of the Xia dynasty, had a beak-like mouth. Yao, a legendary ruler, had eyebrows with eight colors. Tang, a famous ruler of the Shang dynasty, had four elbows on each arm. Yu the Great had three holes in his ears. Li Lou, a legendary figure with sharp eyesight, could see fine details from a distance of ten miles. A minister of the State of Wei cut open his own belly to hide the liver of Duke Yi Gong, a ruler who was overthrown. King Mu of Zhou, a legendary ruler, traveled so extensively that his chariot tracks and horse prints were said to cover the entire world, a total distance of one hundred million miles. On the islands of the sea, Hai Ruo, a sea deity, resides. Every August, a log raft appears. This has happened year after year without fail. One person carried provisions and went aboard the raft, and upon reaching a place, he saw someone drinking water from the river and the Weaving Maid. He asked the man where he was, and the man replied, "You can return and ask Shu's Yan Junping; he will know." The person returned and consulted Yan Junping. Yan Junping said, "On a certain day in a certain month, a guest star appeared in the constellations of Dou and Niu. Calculating the time, it was you." The person then realized he had followed the log raft to the Heavenly River.

Ehuang and Nüying accompanied Emperor Shun on his travels. When they reached the Xiang River, they heard that Shun had died in Cangwu. They wept, and their tears fell on the bamboo of the Xiang River, which turned into spotted patterns. Gong Sun Lü, a minister of the State of Wei during the Spring and Autumn period, had a face that was three chi long and three cun wide. He was a virtuous minister of the State of Wei. During the Eastern Han Dynasty, Zhao Bo翁 once took a nap during the day. His grandchildren played on his stomach and put seven plums into his navel. The plums rotted, and the juice flowed out. His family thought he was going to die. Later, the plum pits came out, and he had no problem. Liu Yao, whose courtesy name was Yongming, had a beard with a hundred strands, each five feet long. Emperor Jing of Zhongshan during the Han Dynasty had one hundred sons and daughters. His descendants have spread out until today. When asked, they are all descendants of Liu. In the Northern Zhou Dynasty, three daughters of Dugu Xin became empresses. Each gave birth to an emperor of the Zhou, Sui, and Tang Dynasties. The eldest gave birth to Emperor Wu of Zhou, the second to Emperor Yang of Sui, and the third to Emperor Gaozu of Tang. Emperor Changyi of Han reigned for twenty-seven days and accumulated a total of one thousand four hundred and twenty-seven evils. He was deposed by Huo Guang. Every time Xiang Yu shouted, ten thousand people's hands and feet would fall off. This is a hyperbolic description of his intimidating presence. King Xuan of Jin, Sima Yi, looked back and saw himself being abandoned. This is a metaphorical expression indicating his sense of betrayal or loss. During the Sui Dynasty, Mai Tietang could travel one thousand miles in one night. He left Luoyang at night to go to Song Prefecture to steal, and returned by morning. The people of Song saw the things he stole and arrested Mai, but he was rescued by officials, and he confessed his guilt.

Cheng Gan of Huainan, originally a wealthy man, had all his possessions burned and destroyed by water and fire over three years. His wife, Ma Shi, gave birth to twins for eight consecutive years, totaling sixteen children, and they supported themselves by begging in the market. Emperor Wu of Liang, despite being a ruler, once renounced his worldly life and became a monk at Tongtai Temple for three years. Guo Ziyi of the Tang Dynasty was appointed as the Grand Secretary and served for twenty-four terms. He earned a monthly salary of 20,000 strings of coins, supported by 2,000 people, and received 500 shi of cooked food and horse fodder. Dehuang, who reached the highest rank, was connected to eight dynasties of emperors: she was the granddaughter-in-law of Emperor Suzong, the niece of Emperor Dezong, the daughter-in-law of Emperor Shunzong, the wife of Emperor Xianzong, the mother of Emperor Muzong, and the grandmother of Emperors Jingzong, Wenzong, and Wuzong. Li Guangli pulled out his sword and stabbed a mountain stone, causing springs to gush forth. Meng Ye of the Eastern Han Dynasty weighed a thousand catties, and the emperor suspected his own weight, so he hung him from the rafters with a large scale. Meng Ye said, "Your Majesty, weigh me on the scale, please." Though his flesh weighed a thousand catties, his wisdom weighed not even an ounce. Gan Bao's mother was jealous, and when she buried her father, she secretly pushed a maid into the tomb. After more than ten years, when she died and was buried with her husband, the tomb was opened, and the maid was found lying on the coffin. After a long time, she revived. When asked, she answered as usual, obeying commands without hesitation. In the early Tang Dynasty, the monk Xuanzang traveled to the West to fetch Buddhist scriptures. Upon returning, he intended to inscribe the date on the wall of Vimalakirti's room. He dipped his brush to write but, after walking several hundred steps, never reached the wall.

A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. In the Tang Dynasty, Li Zisheng's wife Zheng died without illness, and on her deathbed, she told Li Zisheng, "I will not leave your side, and after eighteen years, I will become your kin again." Soon after, Li Zisheng, who was nearly seventy, remarried into the Cui family. She often remembered events from her previous life and later gave birth to a son before dying. During the Western Jin Dynasty, Zhang Gui was the governor of Bao Han, and his concubine gave birth to a daughter, a dragon, and a bird on the same night. One night, Jia Bizi dreamed of a person with an extremely ugly face, who said to him, "Do you want to change it? Is that possible?" In the dream, Jia Bizi seemed to agree slightly. Upon waking, he looked in the mirror and was shocked to see that he looked exactly like the person in his dream. His family members saw him and all ran away. What was strange was that he held a pen in each hand, writing on paper, and both writings had logical structure. When he explained it slowly, his relatives finally believed him. During the Wei Dynasty, the Lingyun Pavilion was completed, and a craftsman mistakenly nailed the signboard. Emperor Wen ordered a rope to be used to lift Wei Dun up, who wrote three characters and then descended. In a short moment, his hair and beard turned completely white. At the end of the Jin Dynasty, a child named Zhao, who was eight years old, suddenly grew abnormally one night, reaching eight feet tall, with a full beard, and died three days later. In the Tang Dynasty, Huizhi had a tangerine tree in his courtyard in Jingzhou. One of the fruits on the tree was exceptionally large, and it was unique. He invited guests to gather and was about to eat it when he cut it open and found a red snake coiled inside. During the reign of Emperor Dezong, there was a woman from Sanyuan named Wang Dan'niang, who could balance on a pole while carrying eighteen people on her head.

In the region of Shaoyang, there was a man who tended cattle. One day, a cow licked his arm, and it turned as white as jade. Delighted by this, he exposed his body to the cow, allowing it to lick him all over, and his entire body turned white. A few days later, the man suddenly died. His family, filled with resentment, killed the cow and called the village community to eat it together. All who ate the cow died on the same night, numbering in the dozens. Yi, the legendary archer, burned an elixir of immortality. Once the medicine was completed, his wife, Chang'e, stole and ate it, then fled into the moon. The *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas) describes a country called the Land of the Large-Eared People, where its inhabitants sleep with one ear as a pillow and the other as a quilt. During the reign of Jia Dan, the military governor of Huazhou, there was a woman in Suzao County who was disrespectful to her mother-in-law. The mother-in-law was very old and blind. In the morning, the woman wrapped dog feces in food and gave it to her mother-in-law. When the mother-in-law ate it, she felt something strange. The woman's son returned from a long journey, and the mother-in-law asked him, "What was it that the woman gave me to eat?" The son cried out to the heavens. Soon after, thunder and lightning struck, as if someone had cut off the woman's head and replaced it with a dog's. Jia Dan ordered her body to be dragged through the territory to warn the unrighteous. People of the time referred to her as the "Dog-Headed Wife." During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, the worker An Jinzang protected Emperor Zhongzong from rebellion. To prove his loyalty, he held a knife to his own abdomen, causing his five organs to fall to the ground. Later, doctors were sent to put the organs back and sew them up with fine mulberry thread. After a night, he revived. When Emperor Xuanzong ascended the throne, he posthumously honored An Jinzang as Duke of Dai. There was a woman named E Jianti, who swallowed an egg and gave birth to Houji (Posterior Ji). During the Eastern Han Dynasty, there was a man named Lu Su. In a year of famine, the governor distributed food to the poor. After the distribution was complete, he asked the recipients to name themselves. A total of six hundred people received food, and all their names were recorded without error.

plaintext King Helu of Wu died, and three thousand people were buried alive as sacrifices for his burial. Emperor Wu of Han had a concubine named Zhao, whose hand was originally clenched. After she was taken as a concubine, her hand uncurled. Wang Xizhi often wrote the text "Inscription for the Temple of Emperor Yuan of Jin" on wooden tablets, and the ink penetrated the wood to a depth of eight points. Wang Rong could look directly at the sun without feeling dizzy. Lian Po could eat a whole peck of rice and ten pounds of meat in one meal. Tao Kan had a fortune-teller examine his hand. The fortune-teller said, "The writing on the palm reaches the fingertips, indicating the rank of Three Excellencies, a position of great honor." Tao Kan pricked his fingers with a needle, and blood flowed out, forming the character 'public' (公) on the wall. During the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang, the Governor of Henan, Xiang Li Zao, examined the magistrate of Luoyang, Miao Deng, who had a tail two feet long. The *Shen Yi Jing* (Book of Strange Things) describes a creature named Li Zi Ang, which is seven inches long and can travel a thousand miles in a day. One day, it was swallowed by a sea bird and survived for three years in its stomach. King Zhou of Shang built a wine pool that was wide enough to sail a boat. After Zhang Shaoping's death, his wife, Lady Tian, lived as a widow for many years. One day, she dreamed of a man descending from the sky, who pressed on her abdomen and caused her to become pregnant. She said, "I conceived without a husband; people will surely abandon me." She moved to Dai and settled in the east. On the first day of May, she gave birth to a son. Because she lived in the eastern part of Dai, she named him Dong Fang Shuo. Some say he was the essence of Jupiter, skilled in many things, and knowledgeable in all fields. Liu Bei, the founder of Shu Han, could see his own ears.

Zou Yan, by playing his flute, could transform a cold valley into one that grew millet and sorghum. Jing Fang, through his mastery of the flute, changed his surname from Li to Jing, having gained fame for his musical skills. Later, he was executed in public. Niu Ai fell ill for three months and transformed into a tiger, then ate the tiger and transformed back into a human. When he was a tiger, he did not know he was a human; and when he became a human again, he did not know he had been a tiger. Shen Baoxu wept at the Qin court for seven days and seven nights without food, begging for troops to save the state of Chu. Qin was moved by his plea and lent him troops to aid Chu. In the Later Zhou, there was a man surnamed Chen who married at age twenty, and his wife was the same age. By the time he was forty, they had children, grandchildren, great-grandchildren, and so on, with over five hundred descendants within a century. All of them, young and old, were killed by Emperor Yang of Sui during his campaign against Goguryeo. During the reign of Emperor Ling of Han, a woman from Hedong ate her husband, and a man from Henan ate his wife. During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. Emperor Wen of Wei once wrote in his work *Dian Lun* that there were no such things as a knife that could cut jade or cloth that could be washed in fire. Shortly after, foreign envoys presented these two items, and Emperor Wen of Wei then destroyed his work *Dian Lun*. There was once a woman named Mulan who disguised herself as a man to take her father's place in the army. She wore military attire for thirteen years, and her fellow soldiers did not know she was a woman. Yang Xinglian, a skilled craftsman from Shu, once carved a wooden statue of a monk that would beg for money in Yizhou. When the money reached fifty coins, the statue would automatically pour the money into a bottle and say, "布施" (alms).

In ancient China, there was a man named Huang An, whose origins are unknown. He often sat on a turtle that feared sunlight. The turtle appeared once every two thousand years, and Huang An had seen it five times. In the third year of Fu Jian's reign, a phoenix gathered at the east hall. Fu Jian wanted to grant amnesty in the country, but no one knew about it. Suddenly, a child appeared in the market, wearing a red scarf over his head, and announced, "The emperor has granted amnesty." Fu Jian investigated further and learned that the child had been transformed from a green fly that had stood on the tip of a pen, flew away, and then changed into a child to inform the people in the market. Yu the Great, while controlling the floods, crossed the Qiantang River during a severe storm. Two flowered snake dragons carried his boat past. His attendants were afraid, but Yu remained calm and fearless. Wang Jun, during his campaign against Wu, built warships on the Shu River. These ships were 240 steps long and had a horse-riding floor on top. The grandeur of these ships was unparalleled in ancient times. During the middle of Emperor Dezong of Tang's reign, there was a beggar named Jie Ruhai. His hands fell from his arms, and his feet fell from his shins. He was skilled at playing ball games and dice games, and also at sword dancing and counting pills. He had two wives and several children. Until the end of the Yuanhe period, he was still alive, and thousands of people gathered daily in the theater in Chang'an to watch him. According to the "Record of Dunhuang," after King Fan died, a thief opened his tomb and saw King Fan playing dice with someone. King Fan gave wine to the thief, who drank it in fear. The thief then saw someone pulling a copper horse out of the tomb. At night, a spirit arrived at the city gate and said he was sent by King Fan. The spirit informed the thief that someone had opened the tomb, and the thief's lips were marked with wine. The spirit instructed the thief to go to the tomb, where he could verify and capture the culprit. The thief entered the city, and the gatekeeper bound and questioned him, confirming the spirit's instructions.

During the Tang Dynasty, Li Bo served as a general in Huai West. In the twelfth year of the Yuanhe era (817 AD), he sent a message of surrender and returned to his own country. Pei Du defeated Wu Yuanji, entered his city, and the Han army committed acts of violence against women, stripping them of their clothing until they were naked. Li Bo's newly married wife, Jiang, was five months pregnant when she was seized by unruly soldiers. They cut into her abdomen with a knife, and Jiang lost consciousness and fell to the ground. Upon returning home, Li Bo saw her with a wound that was more than a foot long. He wrapped her in his own clothing. That night, she revived and was treated with miraculous medicine, eventually recovering. She gave birth to a son after ten months. The imperial court granted an official position to one of Li Bo's sons. His son, named Xingxiu, was over thirty years old and served as the military governor of Nanhai. After his term ended, he returned home and died on the road. Han Yu, the Governor of Zhe West, enforced strict orders. At that time, Chen Shaoyou served as the military governor of Huainan. People who had grievances that could not be resolved went to Han Yu, and he would always resolve them. Money from the area west of Zhejiang was being transported across the river when it was drowned by a sudden storm. Boatmen recruited workers to dredge it out, but two units of currency could not be found. The workers filled the amount with additional money. Han Yu personally went to the crossing point to inspect the situation. He blamed the river god and pointed to the money, saying, "This money is dry; it was not found in the water." He asked the officials, who reported the truth. Han Yu then cast a written accusation and scolded the river god. Suddenly, the two units of currency floated to the surface, and he retrieved them.

Emperor Xuanzong played the jiegǔ. In the early days of the Tianbao era, the spring weather was very warm, but the plants had not yet blossomed. He then ordered the drum to be placed on the palace steps and played it, causing the flowers on the trees to bloom. The emperor said to those around him, "Before the melody ends, the flowers bloom brilliantly. Am I not considered a sage?" Duan Ling loved alcohol and often appeared unclothed, without any clothing. People saw him and scolded him. Duan Ling said, "I consider the heavens and earth as my house and the rooms as my clothing. Since you have nothing to do, why should you enter my room?" The people laughed and left. In the fifth year of the Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty, the court astronomer secretly reported, "There are ominous signs in the heavens, indicating a severe disaster." Emperor Xuanzong was greatly alarmed and asked, "What is the omen?" The court astronomer replied, "There will be 38 prominent individuals who will die unjustly on the same day. The newly appointed jinshi (imperial examination graduates) correspond to this number." One of them was Li Meng, the son-in-law of a noble princess. The emperor, unable to avoid it, told her about the matter and secretly instructed her, "Whenever there are large banquets, keep your beloved son-in-law at home." The princess lived in Zhaoguo Li. At the time, there was a grand musical performance with music spreading far and wide. The Qujiang River overflowed, and several boats were connected together, with all the jinshi gathered. Li Meng heard about it and climbed over the wall to join the festivities, much to the delight of the crowd. As he was boarding the boat, moving towards the pond, a sudden storm arose, causing the boats to sink. The performers and boatmen were so numerous that they could not be counted, and all 38 people died in the disaster.

During the Tang Dynasty, Liu Bi, upon passing the imperial examinations and receiving an official position, visited a fortune teller named Hu Lu Sheng to have his future divined. Hu Lu Sheng, who was completely blind, cast a hexagram for Liu Bi to determine his official prospects. The fortune teller foretold that within twenty years, Liu Bi's career would flourish in the southwestern region but warned that he would not have a good end. Liu Bi left a bundle of silk with Hu Lu Sheng as a gift. Later, Liu Bi left his official robes and served under Wei Linggong in Western Sichuan, eventually rising to the rank of Grand Censor and becoming a military advisor. Twenty years later, Wei Linggong fell ill and died. Liu Bi was sent to report to the court and request additional troops from Eastern Sichuan. However, the imperial edict was not granted. Liu Bi then dressed in plain clothes and rode alone to visit Hu Lu Sheng again. After casting another hexagram, Hu Lu Sheng interpreted the result and said, "Twenty years ago, I once divined for someone who received the hexagram 'No Misfortune Followed by Following.' Now, you have received the same hexagram. Is it not the same person?" Liu Bi hesitantly agreed. Hu Lu Sheng warned him, "If you recognize the person, disaster is imminent." Liu Bi did not fully believe the warning and returned to Sichuan, where he ultimately rebelled. Emperor Xianzong of Tang captured him and executed him in the straw street.

Tang Dynasty's He Zhizhang, a native of Yongcheng, Huiji. He passed the imperial examination and was selected as a jinshi, serving as Assistant Minister of Rites, Chief Librarian, and Tutor to the Crown Prince and other Princes. He was known for his unrestrained and free-spirited nature and his extraordinary imagination. In his eighties, he wrote poetry while drunk and asked his attendants, "How much paper do we have?" They replied, "We have ten sheets." He then wrote a petition for retirement, requesting to return to his hometown. The emperor, the Crown Prince, and other princes all wrote farewell poems, and he was bestowed the area of Yuezhou and Tianqu as a gift. Confucius was traveling through Shaoling Plain when he heard a woman crying very sorrowfully. He sent Zigan to ask her, "Why are you crying so sadly?" The woman replied, "Earlier, when cutting firewood, I lost an ornament." Confucius asked again, "Losing an ornament while cutting firewood is common, but why are you crying so sadly?" She answered, "It is not because I am grieving over losing one ornament. It is because I cannot forget the past."

During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, the recluse Sun Simiao resided on Mount Song practicing Daoism. At that time, there was a severe drought, and an imperial decree selected several thousand monks and disciples with good moral conduct from Luoyang to gather at Tian Gong Temple to lecture on the *Rénwáng Jing* in order to pray for rain. Among the assembly, there were two elderly men with white beards and eyebrows. The lecturer, Master Tan Lin, sent someone to invite the two old men and said, "After the lecture, you may visit a courtyard." Upon arrival, he asked where they had come from. The two old men replied, "We are the spirits of the Yellow and Luo rivers. We heard that your words would bring about transformation." Lin asked, "Do the Buddha and the Bodhisattva know about this?" They answered, "How could they not know? But to bring rain, one must have a divine mandate from heaven. We dare not act on our own." Lin then asked, "What should we do?" The two old men said, "There is a Daoist who can write a memorial to heaven, and then it will rain heavily. We can help with this." Lin then went inside to inform the empress. Empress Wu Zetian sent envoys to Mount Song to summon Sun Simiao. That evening, after Sun Simiao presented a memorial in the inner palace, it rained heavily. Sun Simiao did not take credit for himself but returned to the lecture hall and spoke to Lin, saying, "I have cultivated my mind for fifty years, yet the heavens do not know me. Why is that?" He then asked the two old men. The two old men replied, "If you do not benefit humanity, how could you ascend to immortality?" Thus, Sun Simiao returned to Qingcheng Mountain in Sichuan and compiled the *Qian Jin Fang*, thirty volumes in total. After completing it, he ascended to heaven during the daytime.

During the Battle of Jiankang, Jiang Shi's Minister of Works, Bian Hu, died. He was buried in Shangyuan County. Later, tomb robbers opened his tomb and found that his hair and beard were white, his face looked as if he were alive, his hands were clenched into fists, and his nails had penetrated his palms. Emperor An of Jin granted ten thousand coins to have him reburied. Zhi Yu's *Yaozhu* (Essential Annotations) records: During the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin, Peng Quan served as a high-ranking official. The emperor asked about the meaning of "maotou." Peng Quan replied, "In the Qin dynasty, there was something strange that could destroy mountains and rivers but was feared by the 'maotou.'" The emperor then ordered the Tiger Guards to wear "maotou" to protect him. Pei Zhang of Hedong was the son of Pei Zou, who once governed Jingzhou. Men Seng Tan Zao was a monk with high spiritual attainments who could predict good and bad omens. When Pei Zhang was young, he was highly regarded by Tan Zao, who predicted that he would surpass his father in rank and reputation. When Pei Zhang came of age, his father married him to a woman from the Li family. After thirty years, Pei Zhang went to serve in Taiyuan, leaving his wife in Luoyang and not visiting her, taking another woman with him. Pei Zhang's first wife felt her fate was unfortunate, so she dressed simply, wore her hair in a simple style, read Buddhist scriptures, and ate a vegetarian diet. After another ten years, Yan Jing moved from Jingzhou to govern Taiyuan, and Tan Zao followed him. Pei Zhang met Tan Zao and reminisced about the past. Tan Zao was surprised and exclaimed for a long time, saying, "Fifty years ago, I always said that you would become a nobleman. Now it seems all is lost, why is this?" Pei Zhang revealed to Tan Zao that he had abandoned his first wife for another woman. Tan Zao explained, "Your first wife complained to the heavens about you, and you were punished for your sins." Ten days later, Pei Zhang was killed by his subordinates who cut open his abdomen in a bathtub, causing his internal organs to fall out and leading to his death.

During the Zhenguan era of the Tang dynasty, Zhang Baocang served as the Commandant of the Imperial Guards. Once, after finishing his duties, he returned to Liyang and encountered a young man hunting and feasting on freshly killed game. Leaning against a tree, Zhang Baocang sighed deeply, saying, "At seventy years old, I have never tasted such a feast of wine and meat. How pitiable!" Beside him stood a monk who pointed and said, "Within sixty days, you will be promoted to a third-rank official. There is no need to lament." The monk then vanished. Zhang Baocang found this strange and immediately returned to the capital. Emperor Taizong was suffering from dysentery, and none of the doctors could cure him. He then issued an edict to the officials around him, promising a generous reward to anyone who could treat his illness. Zhang Baocang, who had once suffered from this disease, submitted a prescription for "milk and long pepper decoction." The emperor took it and was immediately cured. The edict was then issued to the prime minister, granting Zhang Baocang a fifth-rank official position. Wei Zheng hesitated and did not submit the proposal for over a month. When the emperor's illness recurred, he asked the officials, "Did I not previously take the milk and long pepper decoction and find it effective?" They were ordered to prepare it again, and after one dose, the emperor was once more cured. The emperor then asked, "I once ordered that the person who submitted the prescription be granted a fifth-rank official position, but I did not see it carried out. Why?" Wei Zheng, fearing retribution, replied, "At the time of the edict, I did not know whether the person was a civil or military official." The emperor became angry and said, "If you can treat the prime minister, there is no reason not to grant him a third-rank position. I am the Son of Heaven, how could I not grant you a promotion?" He then sternly said, "Grant him a third-rank civil official position." Zhang Baocang was immediately appointed as the Minister of Ceremonies, exactly sixty days after the monk's prophecy.

Tao Hongjing lived in seclusion on Maoshan Mountain. Whenever Emperor Wu of Liang had important matters, he would send urgent imperial decrees to consult Tao Hongjing. At the time, people referred to Tao Hongjing as the "Chancellor of the Mountains." Zhang Sengyao, a renowned painter of the Liang Dynasty, served as the governor of Wu County. Whenever Emperor Wu thought of his sons who were stationed in the provinces, he would send Zhang Sengyao to travel by official means to paint their likenesses, which were so lifelike that they seemed as if the subjects were present. Once, Zhang Sengyao painted a Buddha and Confucius along with his ten most prominent disciples in the Tianhuang Temple in Jingzhou. The emperor asked, "Why are you painting these Confucian figures within the Buddhist temple?" Zhang Sengyao replied, "We will rely on these paintings in the future." When the Northern Zhou Dynasty persecuted Buddhism, this temple was spared because it housed the Confucian sages. Zhang Sengyao also painted four dragons in the Anle Temple in Nanjing, but he did not paint their eyes. When people asked him why, he said, "If I paint their eyes, they will fly away." Many people thought this was absurd and insisted that he paint their eyes. Immediately, thunder and lightning struck, and two of the dragons flew up into the sky. The other two, which were not painted, remained on the wall. Zhang Sengyao's skill in painting was truly divine.

晋何曾，家法修整，年五十以上，每见妻，必正衣冠，自坐面南，妻坐面北。上酒酬酢，一岁不至再三。年八十而终焉。 During the Jin Dynasty, He Zeng was known for his strict family discipline. When he was over fifty years old, he always dressed formally and sat facing south when greeting his wife, who sat facing north. They would drink and toast sparingly, no more than three times a year. He lived to be eighty years old and then passed away. 唐贞元初，河南少尹李则卒，未殓。有一朱衣人投刺申吊，自称苏郎中。既入，哀恸尤甚。俄顷，亡者遂起，与之相搏，家人子弟惊走出堂。二人闭门殴击，抵暮方息。孝子乃敢入，见二尸并卧一床，长短、形状、姿貌、鬓髯、衣服一无差异。于是聚族不能定识，遂同棺葬之。 In the early years of the Tang Dynasty's reign of Emperor Dezong, Li Ze, the deputy governor of Henan, died but was not yet buried. A man in red clothing presented a card to express condolences, identifying himself as a Langzhong (an official title). Upon entering, he was extremely grief-stricken. Shortly after, the deceased suddenly got up and fought with him. The family members and children were so shocked that they ran out of the house. The two closed the door and continued to fight until dusk. The son then dared to enter and saw two bodies lying side by side on a bed, indistinguishable in height, shape, appearance, hair, and clothing. The family could not identify which body was the deceased, so they buried them together in one coffin. 汉武陈皇后，本其姑公主鼓女也，色衰，弃后宫，乃以黄金五百斤赠司马相如，令作赋。赋成，帝见之，再得宠幸。 Emperor Wu's consort, Tanchao, was originally the daughter of a princess. When her beauty faded, she was discarded from the imperial harem. She then gave 500 gold pieces to Sima Xiangru, a famous writer, to compose a poem. The poem was well-received by the emperor, and Tanchao regained his favor. 侯景常有一瘤，如小龟。每战胜，龟则起。及其败死之日，瘤入一寸。 Hou Jing, a notorious general during the Southern and Northern Dynasties, had a tumor shaped like a small turtle. The tumor would rise whenever he won a battle. When he was defeated and died, the tumor disappeared. 东汉耿秉镇抚西边，单于匈奴多怀其恩，闻秉卒，有嫠面流血哀痛者，举国发丧。 During the Eastern Han Dynasty, General Geng Bing pacified the western frontier and earned the gratitude of many Xiongnu chieftains. Upon hearing of Geng Bing's death, many people mourned, some even cutting their faces and shedding tears, leading to a national mourning period.

In the early years of the Yuanhe era of the Tang dynasty, there was a man from Tianshui named Zhou who traveled as a guest in  and passed through Zhongbu County. The local officials held a banquet, and a prisoner was captured and brought to the banquet. The prisoner's crime was not very serious, and the officials wished to release him. Zhou was drunk and therefore persuaded to impose additional punishment, so the prisoner was beaten with a stick twenty times. After several months, Zhou went out of the frontier and traveled up to Lusi Pass. On the road, he met a man who invited him for a conversation. As evening fell, the man invited Zhou to his home, which was several miles off the road. They drank wine together, and the man then asked, "Do you recognize me?" Zhou replied, "I have never met you before. This journey is indeed unfamiliar." The man said, "Last month in Zhongbu County, I met you and suffered a wrongful punishment. I had no grudge against you, but because of your persuasion, I was given a severe punishment." Zhou immediately stood up and apologized. The man said, "I have been waiting for you for a long time, did not expect to clear this small disgrace here." He then ordered his attendants to drag Zhou into a room. The room had a large pit, about thirty feet deep, filled with dozens of hu of wine lees. They stripped him of his clothes and pushed Zhou into the pit. He ate the lees when hungry and drank the juice when thirsty, living in darkness and confusion day and night. After about a month, he was taken out. His nose and forehead were flattened, his limbs, fingers, shoulders, and hips were twisted, all changed from their original state. He was taken out into the wind and suddenly became solid. His voice and tone also changed, and he was considered a lowly servant. His younger brother, who was a censor, went to investigate the prison in Lingzhou. Zhou secretly wrote a report about the previous events and showed it to his brother. His brother reported it to the regional governor Li. As a result, soldiers were sent to investigate, and all the villains were captured, and their gang was destroyed. Even at the time of execution, they did not hide anything. Zhou had undergone several generations of changes.

Zheng Tan served in thirty different positions, never leaving the capital, and immediately ascended to the position of chancellor, until his death. During the reign of Emperor Taizong, Xiao Xun once attended an inner palace banquet. The emperor said, "The one who knows he is the most esteemed in the room should drink first." At that time, Changsun Wuji, Fang Xuanling, and others looked at each other without speaking. Xiao Xun reached out to take the cup. The emperor asked, "What do you have to say?" Xiao Xun replied, "I am the son of a Tianzi from the Liang dynasty, the younger brother of a Huanghou from the Sui dynasty, the Left Shangshu, and the son-in-law of the emperor." Emperor Taizong clapped his hands and was extremely pleased, and the banquet ended with great joy. In the early years of the Zhenguan era of the Tang dynasty, Wang Qiong, the magistrate of Danyang, was demoted after three years of service. Qiong was very angry and saddened, so he carried a hundred thousand coins to visit the Daoist master Ye Lingzhong at Maoshan to seek a talisman to inquire about his fortune. Ye Lingzhong, who was ninety years old, reluctantly wrote the talisman for him. The talisman flew away with the incense smoke, disappearing into the distance. After a meal, it fell to the ground, with red writing at the end, which read, "Accepting a hundred taels of gold, deducting three years of salary; wrongfully killing two people, to be judged after death." One year later, Qiong died suddenly without illness.

During the early Tang Dynasty, there was a monk named Xuanzang who traveled to the Western Regions to obtain Buddhist scriptures, and he was gone for seventeen years. On the day he departed, at Lingyan Temple in Qi Prefecture, there was a pine tree standing in the courtyard. Xuanzang placed his hand on one of its branches and said, "I am going west to seek Buddhism. You may grow westward. If I return, let this branch point eastward so that my disciples may know." As he left, the branch of the tree pointed west each year, growing about ten feet long. One year, it suddenly pointed eastward, and his disciples said, "The Master has returned." They then went west to meet him. Indeed, Xuanzang returned, bringing back six hundred volumes of Buddhist scriptures. To this day, the tree is known as the "Mò Dǐng Sōng" (摩顶松). During the Eastern Jin Dynasty, General Zhao Gu's horse died suddenly, and the general was deeply grieved. His officials dared not disturb him. Guo Pu came to the gate and said, "I can revive this horse." The general immediately summoned him. Guo Pu ordered thirty men to carry long poles and travel thirty miles east, where they encountered a hill and a forest believed to be inhabited by spirits. They scattered and attacked, and soon captured an animal resembling a monkey. They brought it back to the horse and placed it in front of it. The monkey used its nose to breathe on the horse, and the horse jumped up as if alive. Today, monkeys are kept in stables, and this is the reason for it. The *Shuo Yuan* (说苑) states: During the reign of King Tang, there was a severe drought that lasted seven years, and the sand and stones were so hot that they were almost melted. The king then ordered the use of a three-legged tripod to perform a ritual for the mountains and rivers, teaching the ritual words: "Is the government not disciplined? Is the people suffering? Are bribes being offered? Are slanderers flourishing? Are the palaces too grand? Are women exerting too much influence? Why is there no rain?" Before he finished speaking, it began to rain heavily. During the Han Dynasty, Huo Guang installed Emperor Xuan, and the emperor performed the initial offering at the ancestral temple. As he walked, Huo Guang followed behind, and the emperor felt deeply ashamed, as if carrying thorns.

During the Eastern Han Dynasty, Jia Cong served as the governor of Ji Province. His official carriage was adorned with a red curtain, symbolizing his high status. Upon ascending the carriage, Jia Cong lifted the curtain and declared, "As a governor, I should see far and hear widely, investigate and correct good and evil. Why would I hide behind a curtain?" The people heard this and were both shocked and awed. In the Eastern Jin Dynasty, Wu Yin served as the governor of Guangzhou. There was a legendary spring called the Greedy Spring, and anyone who drank from it would become greedy. Wu Yin drank from it and composed a poem: "Ancient people said of this water, a single sip would make one covet a thousand gold. If Yi and Qi were to drink, their hearts would remain unchanged." Additionally, during his mother's mourning period, Wu Yin observed more than the prescribed rituals. Despite his poverty, he could not afford to observe the mourning customs properly. At night, he would often hear two cranes cry out in alarm at midnight, and Wu Yin would rise and cry, never missing the time. Wu Zixu, a famous general, played the flute while begging and ate in the marketplace. A butcher named Ding was skilled in butchering cattle. He would throw the knife and leave no trace of effort. Ding said, "When I first started butchering, I saw nothing but a whole cow. After three years, I no longer saw the whole cow. By now, after nineteen years, the knife is as sharp as if it had just been sharpened, and there is always room for the knife to move." A painter named Cai covered a man's nose with a thin layer of plaster, as thin as a fly's wing, and asked a carpenter named Shi to carve it away. Shi wielded his axe with the speed of the wind, removing all the plaster without harming the nose. Emperor Gaozong of Tang once suffered from headaches and had closed eyes and a confused mind. He summoned a physician. The physician said, "You should prick your forehead with a needle to draw blood, and it will heal." Empress Wu Zetian became angry and said, "The emperor's head is where you will draw blood." She ordered the physician to be beaten. The emperor said, "If it is because of the blood that he gets better, that would be fortunate." The physician then pricked the emperor's forehead, and blood splattered his ceremonial robe. The emperor's eyes then opened, and he was completely healed. Empress Wu Zetian herself wrapped a piece of silk to give to the physician as a gift.

Ruan Ji was known for his carefree and unrestrained nature. There was a daughter of a military officer who was exceptionally beautiful, but she died unmarried before her wedding. Ruan Ji did not know her father or brothers, yet he went to mourn her, expressing his deepest sorrow before returning. Emperor Taizong of Tang had a curly beard that was strong enough to hang a bow. Han Gan of the Tang dynasty was skilled in painting horses. During his leisure time, a person suddenly appeared wearing a black cap and red robe. Han Gan asked, "Why have you come here?" The person replied, "I am a messenger of the underworld. I have heard of your skill in painting fine horses and wish to receive one." Han Gan immediately drew a horse and burned it. A few days later, as he was about to leave, someone bowed and thanked him, saying, "I received your gift of a fine horse, which saved me from the hardship of crossing mountains and rivers. I also have something to repay you." The next day, someone sent him a hundred pieces of white silk, whose origin was unknown, and Han Gan accepted and used them. Before his execution, Xie Lingyun cut off his beard and donated it to a Buddhist temple in Guangzhou. His beard was three feet long and still exists today. According to *Records of Marvelous Things*, Jiang Ziya served as the magistrate of Guantan. King Wen of Zhou had a dream in which a person wept in the middle of the road. He asked the reason, and the person replied, "I am a goddess of Mount Tai, married to a woman of the Western Sea. I must travel with violent storms, and Guantan is on my path, so I dare not use strong winds and heavy rains." King Wen awoke from the dream and summoned Jiang Ziya. Three days later, strong winds and heavy rains indeed passed through the region. Huan Huo of the Eastern Jin dynasty governed Jingzhou. One of his military officers, on the fifth day of the fifth month, captured a young magpie, cut its tongue, and trained it to mimic human speech. After a year, the magpie could speak. Later, during a grand gathering, Huan Huo brought it out and made it learn the conversation of all the guests. One person had a condition affecting their nose, and the magpie quickly flew into a jar and spoke just like the person with the ailment. Everyone at the gathering laughed.

During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, the chancellor Lu Huishen died suddenly without illness. His wife Cui Shi instructed the children not to cry, saying, "Your father's fate has not yet ended, and I know it." She continued, "He was frugal and incorruptible, humble in advancement and modest in retreat, and not a single hair or feather was accepted from bribes. At the same time as Zhang Yan, he was chancellor, but Zhang Yan accepted bribes in mountains, and he is still alive. How could the rewards of frugality and extravagance be empty?" By midnight, Lu Huishen revived. Those around him reported his wife's words to him, and he said, "The principles are indeed different. In the underworld, there are thirty furnaces, and they are constantly stirring the bellows to cast ill-gotten wealth. I have none of that, so how can I match them?" After speaking, he died again. In the Tang Dynasty, Pei Jigong Du was ill in Yongle Lane during the late spring month. Suddenly, he went to visit the Southern Garden and ordered his servants to carry him to the medicinal fence. He said, "I cannot die without seeing this flower, which is sad." He returned home in disappointment. The next morning, it was reported that a cluster of peonies had bloomed early. Pei Jigong Du saw them and died three days later. Jin Sheng Jia was known since his youth. Yu Liang held a grand gathering of officials from the state and its prefectures. Jia was seated far away. Yu Liang asked the governor of Jiangzhou, "I have heard of Sheng Jia. Where is he?" The official replied, "He is here; you can recognize him yourself." Soon, he pointed out, "That man is different from the rest; isn't he Jia?" Yu Liang said, "Yes," and laughed, delighted to have found Jia.

Wang Dun, being a son-in-law of the emperor, went to the toilet, where there were many attendants around him. Wang Dun then removed his clothes and climbed onto the toilet naked, showing no signs of shame or embarrassment. One of the maidservants said, "This person must be capable of committing crimes." Indeed, he later caused chaos. During the reign of Emperor Wu of Song, before he killed Yin Zhongwen, Yin would often look into the mirror and never see his head. After a few days, he was indeed killed by Emperor Wu. During the reign of Emperor Xuan of Han, there were two people found under a stone cover in the Shuzhu Mountain, both bound with shackles. They were taken to Chang'an and turned into stone. Emperor Xuan gathered his ministers to ask about this, but none of them knew. Liu Xiang replied, "These are officials from the ancient state of Xiuyu who committed a great crime against the emperor. The Yellow Emperor did not wish to punish them and exiled them to the Shuzhu Mountain. If there were a wise ruler, they would be released." The emperor did not believe him and accused him of speaking nonsense, putting Liu Xiang in prison. Liu Xiang's son, Liu Xin, volunteered to go out and offered to save his father by saying, "We need a seven-year-old girl to nurse them, and they will turn back into people." The emperor had a girl nurse them, and they indeed turned back into people, able to speak and answer questions just like Liu Xiang had said. The emperor was greatly pleased and appointed Liu Xiang as a Grand Doctor and Liu Xin as the Minister of the Imperial Clan. He asked, "How do you know this?" Liu Xin replied, "It is written in the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*." Han Fangshuo was a manifestation of Jupiter. Since he entered the service of Emperor Wu of Han, the star of Jupiter was not visible in the sky. After his death, the star then appeared.

During the Eastern Han dynasty, Wang Ba was renowned for his kindness towards his soldiers. Whenever one of them died, he would take off his own clothes to dress the deceased. Liu Kun, whose courtesy name was Yueshi, was once surrounded by barbarian cavalry. With no relief army arriving, the city was in great distress. Liu Kun then climbed a tower and emitted a clear whistle. The enemy soldiers heard it and felt sad. At noon, he played the barbarian flute again. The enemy soldiers all shed tears and thought of their homeland. Until late evening, he played it again, and the enemy abandoned the encirclement and left. During the reign of Duke Jing of Song, Mars (荧惑) was said to be "guarding" the heart star (心), which was considered an omen of disaster. Zi Wei, the court astrologer, lamented, "The calamity should befall the ruler, but it can be transferred to the chancellor." The duke replied, "The chancellor is responsible for governing the state, so that is not possible." Zi Wei then suggested, "It can be transferred to the common people." The duke said, "The common people are the foundation of the state, so that is not possible." Finally, Zi Wei proposed, "It can be transferred to the year." The duke replied, "The year is the foundation of the people, so that is not possible." Zi Wei then said, "The ruler has spoken three words of great virtue, and the heavens will surely reward him." As a result, Mars retreated three degrees. During the Zhengyuan era of the Tang dynasty, Li Shigu, a military governor, often held banquets for his officials during his leisure time. One day, an astrologer was invited to sit among the guests. Li Shigu had him examine his officials, including Huangfu Bi and Jia Zhiyan, among ten others. The astrologer replied, "Within ten days, all of you will suffer a great misfortune." He then pointed to a certain official named Wang, saying, "This person should first beware of horse-related disasters." At that time, there was an official named Wei, who was related to Li Shigu through marriage. Wei had moved to a new residence and dug a pond, piling up earth on the side. On top of this, he built a high and spacious pavilion. Once completed, he immediately invited Wei to live there, and they enjoyed it. During a drinking session, the pavilion suddenly collapsed because the soil beneath it was weak and unable to bear the weight. All the guests sitting there broke their hands and feet, but did not die. Wang, however, was struck by a long nail from a horse's harness, which pierced through his brain, and he died immediately.

King Chu of Chu fought against Wu and, after retreating forty steps, suddenly lost his shoe and went back to retrieve it. His attendants said, "Although Chu is poor, it is not as if it lacks even one shoe!" The king replied, "I lament that I could not return with it, having taken it out with me." Thus, the state of Chu had no instances of people abandoning each other. Yang Su of the Sui dynasty was extremely wealthy and luxurious. He had thousands of servants and thousands of women in his rear courtyard dressed in fine silk. In major cities, he owned countless shops and mills, to the point where his wealth was boundless. He was greedy for profit, never satisfied, and was widely criticized for it. Li Shan, originally a servant in the household of Li Yuan, found himself in a dire situation during the Jianwu period when Li's family suffered from a plague, killing all their descendants except for one infant who survived for several weeks. The group of servants wanted to kill the infant to divide the family's wealth. Li Shan secretly took the child and fled to the Xueqiu mountains. When the child reached the age of seven, he was discovered and taken care of by officials. Zhongli Yi, the magistrate, punished the servants and commended Li Shan for his loyalty and filial piety. Later, Li Shan was appointed as a prefect. Cao Cao of Wei faced a shortage of food in his army and ordered the granary manager to use smaller measures to distribute grain. Later, when the soldiers complained, Cao Cao blamed the granary manager and said, "I will execute you to silence the many critics." The complaints then ceased. King Hui of Qin attacked Shu by carving five stone oxen and placing gold behind them, claiming they were divine cattle that could produce gold to be given to the king. The Shu king believed this and sent five thousand laborers to drag a path for the oxen. Qin then sent Zhang Yi to follow and conquer Shu. Yu Gong of Han rebuilt his house, raising the door higher so that it could accommodate carriages and horses. He said, "I have done many good deeds in secret, and my descendants will surely prosper." His son Dingguo indeed became a chancellor.

殷 Zhongkan was known for his frugality and served as the governor of Jingzhou. At every meal, crumbs would fall to the floor, and he would pick them up to eat himself. Yi Yin was ashamed that his ruler fell short of the ideals of Yao and Shun, as if he were being whipped in public. Yu lamented that his father's work remained unfinished and thus retreated to Mount Heng in the south. He sacrificed a horse in honor of his father, looked up to heaven, and let out a shout. Suddenly, he dreamed of a divine figure who introduced himself as the Black Water Messenger, telling Yu, "If you wish to receive my book, you must fast." Yu then fasted for three days. A golden tablet inscribed with jade characters descended, revealing the secrets of flood control. Sun Jian, known as Wentai, fell from his horse during battle, and his whereabouts were lost among the soldiers. His beloved steed entered the camp, fell to the ground, and mourned loudly. The soldiers were astonished and followed the horse, finding Sun Jian hidden in the grass. Wang Can once visited Cai Yong, who rushed to greet him by removing his shoes. Everyone in the room was amazed, and Cai Yong said, "Wang Sun has long possessed extraordinary talent; I will give all my books to him." King Goujian of Yue was defeated by the Wu state at Kuaiji but sought to inspire his people. He would place a gall bladder in a vessel and hang it at his door, tasting it upon entering or leaving, so that his soldiers would not forget their hardships. Duan, the father of Sui's He Ruo Bi, on his deathbed, instructed his son to spit out his tongue, which he then stabbed with a needle until blood flowed to the ground. He warned, "I was destroyed by my tongue; you must be cautious with your words." After his father's death, He Ruo Bi was also killed by Emperor Yang for his words. Liu Yu, when poor and lowly, would cover himself with a cotton quilt and make a fly swatter from a cow's tail. Even after becoming emperor, he did not discard these humble possessions. He instructed his daughter, Princess Pengcheng, to carefully preserve the fly swatter for future generations.

Emperor Wu of Liang was extremely fond of Buddhism, yet he had a benevolent and tolerant nature. He imperially ordered contributions of fine silk fabrics, but prohibited the weaving of patterns of birds and beasts, fearing that when cutting, it might cause harm to living beings. Xue Dading, the governor of Cangzhou, diverted seawater, which benefited fish and salt production, and the people of the town sang about it. At the time, the governor of Yingzhou, Jia Dunshun, and the governor of Jizhou, Zheng Deb

Emperor Guangwu of Han cherished his soldiers, and whenever he wanted to send out a single soldier, his hair and beard turned completely white. On the day of伏, Eastern朔, in the presence of Emperor Wu, pulled out a sword to cut meat. The emperor ordered him to blame himself. Eastern朔 said, "Pulling out a sword to cut meat, how brave it is; he didn't cut too much, how frugal it is; giving it back to his wife, how kind it is." The emperor replied, "Ordering you to blame yourself, you ended up praising yourself instead." He was then granted a measure of wine and a hundred catties of meat, and ordered to give it to his wife. Emperor Mu of Qin lost his favorite horse, and the people of Qishan ate it together. The officials wanted to punish them, but the emperor said, "A gentleman does not harm people for the sake of animals. I have heard that eating horse meat without drinking wine harms people." They were all given wine to drink. Later, they fought against Jin, and three hundred men fought to the death to repay the favor. Wèi  had a nature that was broad and forgiving. He often warned himself, saying, "When people fail to meet expectations, it can be forgiven out of compassion; when someone unintentionally causes harm, it can be resolved through reason." Therefore, he never harbored resentment or anger. An old man from Hanyin carried a jar into the well and carried water to irrigate his garden. Someone taught him to use a shadoof, which required little effort but provided great utility. The old man said, "I would rather tire myself out than use a machine, as it would harm my true nature." Emperor Qin Shi Huang wanted to see the sun, so he built a stone bridge on the coast and commanded spirits to transport materials. He said, "Can I see your form?" The sea god then appeared and said to Emperor Qin's attendants, "My form is very ugly, do not draw my form." Among his attendants, there was a clever one who secretly drew a map with his feet. The god became angry, and the coastline then collapsed. Emperor Qin escaped, barely escaping death, while his attendants were all drowned.

Liyang County had an elderly woman who often did good deeds. Suddenly, a young man passed by her door asking for food. She treated him with great respect. As he was leaving, he told the old woman, "Whenever you go to the county, if you see blood at the threshold, climb the mountain to avoid disaster." From then on, the old woman went to the door every day. An official asked her about the situation, and she replied that the young man had told her. The official then played a trick by painting the threshold with chicken blood. The next day, the old woman saw blood and took the chicken cage up the mountain. That night, the county sank into a lake, which is now known as Liyang Lake in Hezhou. Fu Yi often did not believe in Buddhism. During the reign of Emperor Gaozu, there was a foreign monk from the Western countries who could spit fire to intimidate the crowd. Fu Yi told Emperor Gaozu, "This foreign method is not trustworthy. If the fire can burn me, then he is a saint." Emperor Gaozu tested him by placing the monk on the west side of the hall and Fu Yi on the east side, then ordered the monk to perform his magic. The monk jumped and chanted spells, and fire emerged from his mouth, directly touching Fu Yi. Fu Yi held his tablet and said, "Qian, yuan heng li zhen. Evil cannot harm righteousness." As a result, the fire turned back and burned the monk, killing him instantly. Emperor Xiaowu of Jin wanted to find a husband for Princess Jinling and asked Wang Zijing, "If he can match Liu Zhenzhang and Wang Zijing, that would be enough." Wang Zijing replied, "Xie Kun is not as good as Liu Zhenzhang, but not inferior to Wang Zijing." The emperor agreed. Soon after, the emperor died. Later, the Chancellor Yuan Song wanted to marry his daughter to Xie Kun, and Wang Zijing said, "Do not get too close to the forbidden delicacy." During the early years of Emperor Yuan's crossing of the river, the country was often short of resources. Officials often cooked pork, and the neck meat was especially tender and delicious, becoming a favorite dish for the imperial table. People at the time referred to this as "the forbidden delicacy."

During the Dali era of the Tang Dynasty, there was a county magistrate named Hou Yi in the county of Wan Nian. He was known for his chivalry and righteousness and often harbored traitors. When the censor investigated him, all logical arguments were exhausted, but he still refused to reveal the whereabouts of the traitor. The censor said, "The traitor is hidden in your right knee." Hou Yi then lifted a brick from the floor and struck his own knee, turning to show the censor and saying, "Where is the traitor?" He then placed a metal container with a fire on his stomach, and the smoke and flames rose vigorously, making those around him unable to bear the sight. Hou Yi shouted, "Why not add more charcoal?" The censor was amazed and reported this to Emperor Dali. The emperor summoned him and asked, "Why did you hide the traitor and endure such suffering?" Hou Yi replied, "The traitor is indeed hidden by me, but I have made a promise to him, and it is impossible for me to reveal him even at the cost of my life." Because of this, he was demoted to the position of magistrate in Gao'an, Rui Prefecture. During the reign of King Mu of Zhou, he led a southern campaign. One day, all the soldiers were transformed into monkeys and cranes. The nobles became cranes, and the commoners became monkeys. Chen Sheng wrote the words "Chen Sheng is king" on a piece of red silk and placed it in a fish's belly. Someone bought the fish, cooked it, and found the silk, which surprised him. He then proclaimed Chen Sheng as king. Liu Bei once rode a beloved horse named "Dilu." While staying in Fan Cheng, Liu Biao wanted to kill him during a banquet. Liu Bei fled and fell into the Tan Creek. He then told Dilu, "Today, you must try your best." The horse leaped three meters and helped Liu Bei escape. Ren Gongzi went fishing, using a fishing line that was fifteen  long. He sat by the West Lake and waited for a year without catching anything. One day, he caught a large fish, and the smell of the fish spread throughout the Jing River area.

When Tian Zifang of Qi left, he saw an old horse by the road and sighed deeply, moved by the sight. He asked the driver, "What kind of horse is this?" The driver replied, "It is a horse belonging to the state, tired and no longer used, so it has been released." Tian Zifang said, "When young, it has expended its strength, and when old, it is discarded. A benevolent person would not do this." He then tied up some goods to redeem the horse. The poor scholar, upon hearing this, knew where to return. Wu Qi sucked a wound on a seat, and the mother of the deceased cried, saying, "Last year, Wu Qi sucked his father's wound, and his father died without turning his foot. Now he is sucking his son, and I don't know where he will die." The old man lost his horse, and all the villagers came to mourn. The old man said, "It may not be a misfortune." The next year, a group of horses arrived. People came to congratulate, and the old man said, "It may not be a blessing." The children and servants often rode the horses, and falling and breaking limbs occurred. The villagers came to mourn again, and the old man said, "It may not be a misfortune." Throughout the territory, no one mentioned the character 祜. Even if it had the same sound, they also changed it. The people of Xiangyang erected a weeping stone on Xianshan. #### Volume II Prince Guang of Wu entertained Lord Liao of Wu, ordering Zhuan Zhu to serve. He placed a sword in the belly of a steamed fish and, as the fish was served, drew the sword to stab and kill Lord Liao. Yang Hu, styled Shizi, served as the governor of Jingzhou, and his benevolence reached the common people. Upon his death, the entire territory did not mention the character 祜. Even if it had the same sound, they also changed it. The people of Xiangyang erected a weeping stone on Xianshan.

Wang Yun intended to kill Cai Yong, but Ma Rong said, "Cai Yong is a man of exceptional talent and many skills, as recorded in the Han histories. How can he be harmed?" Wang Yun replied, "Emperor Wu of Han did not kill Sima Qian, allowing him to write a defamatory book that spread later. How can we now allow a sycophantic minister to write, and subject ourselves to his ridicule and criticism?" And so, Cai Yong was killed. Chen Shou of Wei wrote the "Records of the Three Kingdoms." Ding E and Ding Yi both had great fame in Wei. Chen Shou told his son, "Give me a thousand bushels of rice, and I will write a good biography for your father." His son did not give it to him, and so Chen Shou did not write the biography. Wang Meng and Liu Yi both served as Secretaries of the Central Secretariat during the Jin dynasty. When they came to the Song dynasty, they both served as advisors and were promoted to the position of Attendant. People at the time called them "Inner Chamber Guests." Zhao Yao of Han served as a Censor for Zhou Chang. People said to him, "Zhao Yao is a remarkable man, and he will surely replace you as a Doctor." Zhou Chang said, "Zhao Yao is just a clerk. How did he get here?" Later, Zhou Chang became the Chancellor of the State of Zhao. Emperor Gao held the seal of a Doctor and looked at Zhao Yao, saying, "There is no one better than Zhao Yao." And so, Zhao Yao was appointed to the position. The emperor asked Wang YiFu, "Why is it that east of Shouyang there is often flooding, and west of Shouyang there is often drought?" Wang YiFu said, "East of Shouyang, the people of Wu, with the lamentations of a lost state, a powerful nation that once stood as a tripod, lost their position, their sorrow and anger turned to darkness, which accumulated into water, hence the frequent flooding. West of Shouyang, the Central States, having recently subdued the strong Wu, all the treasures were taken in, their hearts filled with ambition and joy, hence the frequent drought."

Chang served as the governor of Henan during a prolonged drought when prayers and sacrifices went unanswered. He then collected and buried over a hundred traveler corpses who had died near the city. Suddenly, it rained heavily, and the crops were abundant. Cao Zhang of the Northern Wei dynasty was known for his free-spirited and unrestrained character. One day, he chanced upon a fine horse and loved it, which its owner cherished. Cao Zhang said, "I have a beautiful concubine who can be exchanged; you may choose her." The horse owner pointed to a courtesan, and Cao Zhang exchanged the horse for her. The horse was named "Bai Gu" (White Hawk). Later, during a hunt, he presented the horse to Emperor Wen. According to the *Wuling Ji* (Records of Wu Ling), during the Eastern Han dynasty, Ma Rong, who was diligent in his studies, dreamed of a forest where the flowers were like embroidered brocade. In the dream, he picked and ate these flowers. Upon waking, he found that he knew all the literary works in the world. People at the time called him the "Embroidered Bag." Cui Bao's *Gujin Zhu* (Notes on Ancient and Modern Times) records that the queen of Qi died in resentment, and her body transformed into a cicada, which then climbed onto a courtyard tree and chirped. Later, the king regretted and would weep upon hearing the cicadas. During the reign of Emperor Xuan of Chen, a man named Yan Tai from Yangzhou encountered a fishing boat while traveling on the river and asked about it; the fisherman said, "There are fifty turtles." Yan Tai paid 500 coins to redeem and release them. After walking several dozen steps, the fishing boat capsized. That night, fifty people dressed in black robes knocked on Yan Tai's door and told his parents, "Your son has brought 500 coins; you may collect them." The coins were all wet. His parents accepted the coins but did not know the reason. Yan Tai returned home and asked about it, and it turned out to be a strange event related to redeeming the turtles. He then built a temple on the site, and the locals called it "Fayin Temple."

Wang Dao's son, Yue, was twenty years old, famous, and held the position of Zhongshu Lang. Wang Dao once dreamed that someone bought Yue for a million coins and led him away in the dream. Wang Dao woke up feeling unhappy and prayed urgently. Soon after, while repairing the wall, they dug up a million coins, which Wang Dao considered ominous and did not use. When Yue fell ill, Wang Dao had another dream where a person wearing armor and holding a knife, calling himself Prince Jiangshan, asked for food. After eating, the person in the dream became angry and told Wang Dao, "Your son has already been sold to someone else." After speaking, Wang Dao woke up. The next day, Yue died. Emperor Jing of Han enjoyed hunting and once saw a tiger but could not catch it, so he prepared fine dishes to sacrifice to the tiger. Emperor Jing then had a dream where the tiger said, "You sacrificed to me, do you want my teeth and skin? I will kill myself, and you can take them." The next day, Emperor Jing went to the mountain and indeed found the tiger dead at the place of sacrifice, and he ordered the skin and teeth to be taken. The remaining flesh all turned into tigers and left. Ma Lüo from the Later Han dynasty, at the age of seventeen, studied in seclusion for nine years, eating only once every three days to sustain his life. The villagers called him "Hidden Dragon." At the age of thirty, Ma Lüo paid homage to Emperor Huan and said, "I am a virtuous person." He was then appointed as Marquis of the Inner Pass and Governor of Guangzhou. Ma Lüo resigned from office and entered the sea, and all evil insects and fierce beasts avoided the path. According to the *Lüshi Chunqiu*, two virtuous men from Qi met on the road and bought wine to drink together. One of them wanted to buy meat, and the other said, "You are also meat, and I am also meat, there is no need to go to the market." They then used knives to cut their own flesh and fed each other. Soon after, both the wine and the meat were finished, and they both died.

In the third year of Emperor Wu of Liang's Dachen era, Hou Jing rebelled and besieged Tai Cheng, cutting off communication with the outside world. Emperor Jianwen and Crown Prince Daqi devised a plan to tie a kite to a message and send it into the air, in an attempt to send an urgent plea for help. Hou Jing's advisors told him, "This must be a form of magical ritual, or else it is a clever device to reach people." He ordered his attendants to shoot it down. When the kite fell, it transformed into birds and flew away, and no one knew where they went. The *Biography of Jing Fang* in the *Book of Han* states: "Jing Fang, on the eve of his execution, told someone, 'After my death, a guest star will enter the well.'" The entire court mourned him. Wang Chong's *Lunheng* records: "During the Han dynasty, Li Zichang, while governing, wanted to know the truth of a prisoner's situation. He carved a wooden figure of the prisoner, dug a pit in the ground, and placed the wooden figure under interrogation. If the prisoner was guilty, the wooden figure would not move; if the prisoner was innocent, the wooden figure would shake its head. The power of spirit and emotion can establish justice, and it moves the divine in such a way." Wang Zini's *Shiyiji* recounts: "During the reign of King Zhao of Yan, the people of Boyi presented a fragrant tea. If burned, the fragrance would linger on clothing for a month. If passed over the ground, the earth and stones would become fragrant. If passed over decaying wood and rotten grass, they would become lush and beautiful. If used to fumigate dry bones, flesh would regenerate." Empress Yang of the Eastern Han dynasty, during the reign of Emperor Ming, was known for her beautiful and colorful face but suffered from a form of mental illness. When she became violent, she would kill people. Only her tutor Meng Zhao, who wrote mournful and sad texts, could calm her. Whenever she read his writings, her madness would subside. People at the time said, "Meng Zhao's writing is a remedy for her madness."

The *Yuxiang Ji* states: During the Western Han dynasty, Liu Ziguang was on a western campaign and, while crossing a mountain, became thirsty with no water available. In the mountains, Liu Ziguang saw a stone figure and asked it, "Where is there water?" The stone figure did not answer, so he drew his sword and cut the stone figure. Shortly after, water suddenly gushed out from the mountain. The two sons of King Huàn of Qi, Róng and Chēn, were born on the same day in April to Lady Yīn. They were also executed on the same day in April in the capital city. In the first year of the Taichang era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Liang, Wang Bao, a farmer from Yueyang, had six hectares of land that all produced fine rice. During the reign of King Lì of Zhou, the Big Dipper and the Three Stars flowed together, and their whereabouts were unknown. After King Lì's death, the two main stars reappeared. The *Search for the Supernatural* states: During the time of the state of Wu, Ge Zuó served as the governor of Héngyáng. There was a large log in the river that damaged boats. If one sacrificed to the log, it would float and become visible; if one did not sacrifice, it would sink and secretly capsize boats. Ge Zuó made dozens of large axes and went to chop it down the next day. That night, the waves were turbulent and alarming. The log floated away and moved off, no longer being a problem in the river. People set up a stele to praise Ge Zuó's virtue. Gan Bǎo's *Search for the Supernatural* states: "The governor of Línglíng, Shǐ Mǎn, had a daughter who liked a clerk. She secretly sent a servant to fetch the clerk's leftover water and drank it, and she became pregnant. By the time she was a year old, the governor had her taken out of the door. The child crawled into the clerk's arms, and the clerk pushed her down to the ground, where she turned into water. After fully understanding the previous events, the governor married his daughter to the clerk.

Sima Yi was promoted to the position of *Sikong*, and that night, someone knocked on his door to request an audience. The person introduced himself as a *Baihu* messenger, dressed in white robes, and took an object from his chest, placing it in Sima Yi's hand. He warned, "Be careful not to open it for two generations, for the tomb will be cut off." After speaking, the messenger disappeared. Sima Yi remarked, "Perhaps this is fate." He then opened the object to see that it was a golden dragon offspring, about three to four inches long, with an inscription on its back that read, "Father and son will receive the heavy fire from me." When Emperor Wu of Wei received the throne through the act of *shouchan* (abdication), the Sima family's tomb was said to be cut off. Later, during the reign of Emperor Yuan of Jin, who crossed the river to establish Jiankang as the capital, the Sima family's fortunes declined. According to *The Records of the Three Gorges*, in the second year of the Shengming era of Emperor Shun of Song (478 AD), a man named Weisheng Liang from the Three Gorges region caught a white fish three feet long in a stream. He placed it in his boat and covered it with grass. When he returned home, he took it out to cook it, and a beautiful woman emerged. She was pure and graceful, around sixteen or seventeen years old. She introduced herself as a woman from Gao Tang who had accidentally transformed into a fish and was now in his possession. Weisheng Liang asked, "Now that you are human, can you be my wife?" She replied, "Fate has willed it so, why not?" They were married for three years. The woman then said, "The number of times is enough; please return to Gao Tang." Weisheng Liang asked, "When will you come back?" She answered, "The feeling cannot be forgotten; if I think of you, you will come back." She visited him three or four times a year, and no one knows what happened to her in the end.

According to the *Cheng Yingyuan Shi Tong*: Cai Yun was a diligent scholar who often gathered firefly light to read. One day, during a storm, Cai Yun lamented, "Is it heaven that does not allow me to accomplish my aspirations?" As he finished speaking, a large firefly appeared near the study window, several times larger than ordinary fireflies. After he finished reading, it flew away. Whenever there was a storm, it would appear. During the reign of Emperor Ling, Huang Ba served as the magistrate of Fengxi. A local person named Chen Lian brought wine and a talking monkey as gifts. Huang Ba asked, "What is this?" Before anyone could respond, the monkey in the bag spoke, saying, "A bowl of wine and I myself." Huang Ba, believing the creature to be magical, opened the bag and released it, and the monkey wept and left. During the Southern Liang Dynasty, Xu Mian served as a high-ranking official. Emperor Wu of Liang entrusted him with state affairs, and he would return home only a few times a month. One day, his dog barked at him upon his return. Xu Mian sighed, "I worry about the state and forget my family, leading to this." The *Yue Jue Shu* states: "After being humiliated by Wu, King Goujian of Yue treated scholars with utmost courtesy, thinking of how to defeat Wu. One day, while out, a frog became angry, and Goujian bowed to it. His attendants asked, 'Why does the king bow to an angry frog?' Goujian replied, 'If the frog is so angry, how can I not bow?' Upon hearing this, the brave men all returned to Yue and helped him defeat Wu."

The *Records of the Supernatural* states: When Feng Ling's wife died, Feng Ling was deeply grieved and lamented, saying, "Why didn't she bear a son before dying!" Soon after, his wife revived. She later became pregnant for ten months and gave birth, but then died. In the twenty-eighth year of Qin Shi Huang's reign, he ascended Mount Tai for the ritual of offering sacrifices to heaven. Halfway up, there was a sudden storm with rain, lightning, and thunder. Beside the road, there were five pine trees, their shade covering several acres. Qin Shi Huang then bestowed upon them the rank of "Five Great Officers." Suddenly, someone was heard speaking from the top of the pines: "Lacking virtue and propriety, and ruling the world without just cause. Why did the emperor bestow titles?" Everyone present heard this, and Qin Shi Huang was displeased. He returned, and soon after, he died at Shaoqiu. Emperor Gaozu of Han often faced battles, where he was exposed to arrows and stones. He was hit by seventy-two arrows, or so it was said, that seventy-two black spots were removed from him. Liu Ji, whose courtesy name was De Feng, studied diligently and worked hard. At night, he burned wood leaves to light his way, as he had no oil lamps. One night, he heard someone calling from outside his window. He went out to see and found five or six men, each carrying a sack, pouring something like elm seeds onto the ground. They said, "We are providing you with provisions for your studies, so do not worry about your work not being completed." The next morning, when he looked, it was all ancient Han coins, amounting to one hundred and seventy thousand. With this, he completed his studies. During the reign of Emperor Mingdi of Song, he held the position of Eastern Palace Attendant. In the late Western Han dynasty, Liu Shengong, upon obtaining the imperial seal and ribbon, saw a meteor fall and wrap around him like a rope. The next day, Liu Penge's general Xie Lu strangled him, just as the meteor had predicted. Cao Cao was unjust, appointing officials such as "Faqiu Zhonglang" and "Moujin Xiaowei." Regardless of whether they were new or old, he ordered the excavation of tombs across the land. Bones were scattered across the fields, and people were all saddened by this. His cruelty and ruthlessness were evident.

The *Cheng Yingyuan Shitong* records: Liu Mu, courtesy name Ziren, once lived in the wilds of the South Mountain. He delighted in the songs of mountain birds and the rustling of wind through the pines, planting fruits and vegetables. The villagers mocked him, often cutting down trees and trampling his garden. Liu Mu said, "I have not wronged others; why should they wrong me?" Soon, two tigers approached his dwelling. When they saw Liu Mu, they wagged their tails. He said, "You come to protect me." The tigers bowed their heads. After several years, Liu Mu passed away, and the tigers departed. Chiyou was an ancient emperor, with eighty-one brothers. They all had copper heads and iron foreheads, eating sand and gnawing on stones, but were ultimately defeated by the Yellow Emperor. According to the *Shenxian Ji*: King Song of Kang took Han Peng's wife because she was beautiful and made Han Peng build the Qingling Terrace, then killed him. His wife requested to attend his funeral and threw herself into the grave, dying there. The king ordered her to be buried on either side of the terrace.
