After a year, each side grew a paulownia tree, which grew large and their branches intertwined. Two birds mourned sadly on top of them. People called this tree the "Tree of Longing." The Yellow Emperor defeated Chiyou, and his tomb is located in Shouchang County, Gaoping, standing seven zhang high. People often held ceremonies there in October, seeing a red vapor like a piece of red silk, which they called the "Chiyou Flag." The *Xijing Zaji* states: When Hong Chengzi was young, he loved learning. Once, a person passed by his door and gave him a piece of jade, as big as a swallow's egg. After swallowing it, he became enlightened and even more intelligent, becoming a renowned scholar throughout the land. Wu Lu Chongzong also received teachings from Chengzi. One day, Chengzi fell ill and spat out the stone. Chongzong took it and swallowed it, and he too became a famous scholar.

In his humble days, Jin's Tao Kan, while mourning the death of his father and preparing for the burial, suddenly lost his ox and did not know where it had gone. He met an old man, who said to him, "Before you, there is a cow lying in the field. If you bury your father there, you will attain the highest rank among ministers." Then, pointing to a mountain, the old man added, "That place is also auspicious, and in the world, it will produce an official of the rank of two thousand *dan*." After speaking these words, the old man vanished. Tao Kan found his ox, and thus changed the burial site. Han's Grand Commandant Yang Zhen was dismissed from office due to his integrity. When he returned to Luoyang, he sighed, saying, "I held a high position, yet I could not punish the cunning minister Fan Feng, nor could I fill the empty treasury." He then drank poison and died. His disciples mourned him, and the emperor praised him. On the day of his reburial, a great bird with a wingspan of thirteen feet gathered before the coffin, lowered its head and wept. After the burial was completed, the bird flew away. At the time, people believed this was a sign of divine response to his loyalty and righteousness. *The Records of Kuaiji* states: On Lan Shi Mountain in Shangyu, where the Daoist master Ge Xuan once lived in seclusion, there was a table that transformed into a deer. When the deer cried out, it was a sign that the county magistrate had committed a crime. Sima Jiao, styled Zi Du, lived in seclusion on Mount Hua for nearly fifty years. Wild animals roamed daily around him, as if they were domesticated. Every time he tended his garden, he would not eat the center of the vegetables, for he believed it would harm the life force of the plant. When the mountain fruits ripened in the four seasons, large birds would carry the big ones, and small birds would carry the small ones, all delivering them to Sima Jiao's study, without end. He sighed, saying, "The birds bring me many fruits, but I will only eat thirty each day." Afterwards, they obeyed his rule. After thirty years, when Sima Jiao died, a hundred kinds of birds gathered in his courtyard, mourning for many days before flying away.

Cao Zhi, the Prince of Wei and a renowned poet, was at odds with Cao Pi, the future Emperor Wen of Wei. After Cao Pi ascended the throne, he once intended to harm Cao Zhi but dared not act without restraint due to their mother's favoritism toward Cao Zhi. Cao Pi then invited Cao Zhi to visit the Hualin Garden, where he drank him to intoxication and secretly ordered his attendants to strangle him. The attendants tried three times to strangle Cao Zhi with a bowstring, but he did not die, and the bowstrings all broke suddenly. Cao Zhi then awoke in alarm. His attendants ran to inform the emperor, and from then on, Cao Pi dared not harm Cao Zhi. According to the *Huayang Guozhi*: In the kingdom of Yelang, there was a woman who was washing clothes by the water's edge. Suddenly, she saw a three-jointed bamboo tube come before her. Hearing a baby cry inside the bamboo, she cut it open and found a boy. She raised him, and when he grew up, he showed great martial talent. He declared himself the Lord of Yelang and took the surname Zhu. In Zichuan, there was a woman named Yan Wenjiang. She was filial and diligent in serving her mother-in-law, going beyond just chopping firewood to also fetch mountain spring water for her mother-in-law to drink. One day, under the basket she was weaving, a spring suddenly gushed forth, clear and refreshing. The people of the time called it "Yan Lady's Spring," and it continues to benefit people to this day. During the Han dynasty, Yang Pu served as the General of Naval Forces. He considered himself highly accomplished and was ashamed to be considered an outsider. He requested to use his personal wealth to relocate the border post to Xin'an, and the emperor granted his request.

《Liezi》states: "Han E passed through the gate of Qi, selling her songs and food. After she finished, her lingering voice echoed around the beam for three days without ceasing. Han E then sang in a prolonged, mournful tone, causing the old and young in the surrounding area to weep and cry, unable to eat for three days. She then sang a long song, and the people of the gate of Qi rejoiced and danced continuously, so they sent her away with generous gifts. Xiang Yu began digging into the tomb of the First Emperor, searching for jewels. Those that were not fully taken, a golden goose flew out of the tomb, and was caught by the netters. During the reign of Emperor Wu of Han in the Yuanfeng period, the country of Fuqi presented purple gold mud. The gold was born in the hot springs. On hot summer days, the waves were always boiling, and birds dared not fly over, nor did the residents dare to cross. The people by the water saw this mud and took it to make objects, which were colored like polished purple gold, and the smooth ones were as soft as mud. It was presented to the Han dynasty, and the emperor took it, always sealing and storing it in a box to ward off evil spirits. Wei Qing and Zhang Qian were both honored with this mud to seal their seals and ribbons. After the emperor died, the purple mud was no longer available. Shi Hu built a tower in front of the Taiwu Hall, forty zhang high, with pearls as curtains and five-colored jade as pendants. Whenever the wind came, it would shock and touch like music in the air. Passersby all looked up and loved it. He also ground various exotic fragrances into powder and scattered them on the building, where the wind dispersed them, creating a fragrant dust called 'fragrant dust.' The *Lüshi Chunqiu* states: There was a person with a bad smell, and his parents, brothers, wives, and children, as well as passersby, all hated him. This person had no place to be, so he went to the sea. There were people on the sea who liked his smell, and they followed him day and night, unable to leave."

During the reign of Duke Wen of Jin, a snake blocked the path. Duke Wen considered this an ill omen and thus changed his governance to cultivate virtue, ordering officials to guard the snake. One of the guards had a dream in which a person killed the snake, saying, "Why should a snake obstruct the path of a sage?" Upon waking, he found the snake already broken. The *Zhuangzi* records: During the reign of Duke Huan of Qi, he went out touring in a marsh. At the edge of the marsh, he saw a creature as large as a wheel and as long as an ape, dressed in purple and wearing a red cap. When it saw people, it would bow its head. Duke Huan said to Guan Zhong, "Is this creature a monster?" Guan Zhong replied, "This is a wēiyí (委蛇), and whoever sees it will become hegemonic." Indeed, Duke Huan later became a hegemon, and his state became the first of the Five Hegemons. Marquis Wuji of Wei was in office when suddenly a hawk chased a dove. The dove fled and hid under Marquis Wuji's desk, and the hawk flew away. He ordered the capture of hawks, catching hundreds of them and arranging them in the courtyard. He asked them, "The one who chased the dove should confess." One hawk confessed its guilt on the ground, and it was then killed, while the rest were released. During the Later Han dynasty, Lu Jingzhu was born with a cluster of white hair on his neck, numbering forty-nine strands. He died forty-nine years later. Also during the Later Han dynasty, Pei Anzu often rested under a big tree. One day, a fierce bird chased a rooster, which, in panic, flew into Pei Anzu's arms and suddenly hit a tree and died. Pei Anzu mourned the rooster, placed it in a shaded area, and soon after, the rooster revived and flew away. That night, he had a dream in which a man with a grand appearance bowed to thank Pei Anzu, saying, "I repay your protection, so I come to express my gratitude." Pei Anzu died at the age of eighty.

During the Eastern Han dynasty, Véng Hóng served as the governor of Lín Huái. While performing the spring ritual, two white deer appeared alongside his carriage. Véng Hóng found this strange, and his subordinate, Huáng Guó, said, "When a deer appears beside the carriage of a high-ranking official, it is an omen that the official will become a chancellor." Later, Véng Hóng indeed rose to the position of Taivèi. In the Song dynasty, Yì Qìng, the son of Prince Cáo Dào Lián, was ill in Guāng Líng. One day, while eating porridge, a white rainbow entered the room and tried to eat his porridge. Yì Qìng threw the bowl down the steps, and the rainbow then made a sound like wind and rain, shaking the courtyard gates. After a long time, the rainbow disappeared. According to the *Lì Nǚzhuàn* (Biographies of Exemplary Women): "Táo Dázǐ served as the chancellor of Táo, but his administration was neglected, and his family became increasingly wealthy. His wife, holding her child, wept. Her mother-in-law asked, 'Why are you crying?' She replied, 'I have heard that the black leopard of the South Mountain, when it rains for ten days, does not eat, in order to make its fur glossy and beautiful, thus gaining its majestic appearance and avoiding harm. Now, my husband neglects virtue but his family grows richer, and disaster will surely come.' After a year, Táo Dázǐ was killed." In the pseudo-Song state, Rì Shì had a concubine named Zhang who had a beautiful but ominous appearance, and Rì Shì favored her. One day, she transformed into a large spotted snake, over ten feet long, and was sent to the imperial garden. That night, she returned and slept under his bed. Rì Shì was terrified and had her killed. Later, there was a lady named Zheng, whom Rì Shì also favored, and she too transformed into a female tiger. One night, she ate three of Rì Shì's concubines. Soon after, Rì Shì was killed by Huán Wēn.

Wu Daozi was skilled in painting the divine. During the Yuan period, General Pei Ji, while in mourning for his mother, went to Wu Daozi and requested that several walls at the East Capital Tian Gong Temple be painted with gods and ghosts to aid in the afterlife. Wu Daozi replied, "I have stopped painting for a long time. If General you wish, perform for me a dance of entangled swordplay, perhaps through your fierce effort, I may gain access to the underworld." Pei Ji then took off his mourning clothes, dressed as he usually did, and rode his horse like flying, spinning left and pulling right, throwing his sword into the clouds, which soared dozens of feet high, like lightning shooting down. Pei Ji extended his hand to catch the hilt, and the sword plunged through the air and descended. Thousands of spectators were filled with awe. Wu Daozi then picked up his brush and painted the walls, and in the blink of an eye, evil spirits emerged, a sudden wind arose, creating a spectacular sight in the world. This was the most satisfying painting Wu Daozi had ever done. Prince Wang Kai of the Jin dynasty had a cow named "Eight Hundred Li," which he always polished its horns. Wang Wu Zi once bet against it with gold, and by chance, he hit it. He told his attendants, "It is possible to cut out its heart and roast it." When they did, he ate a piece and stopped. After General Jiang Wei of Shu died, his abdomen was cut open, and his gallbladder was found to be as large as a basket. Zuo Si composed the *Three Capitals Fu*, placing paper and ink in his doorways, walls, and latrines, and it took him ten years to complete it. Han Zhang Cang, old and toothless, drank human milk and lived over a hundred years. He often felt grateful to Wang Ling, and after his mother's death, he served Wang Ling's wife, attending to her day and night as if she were his mother.

During the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang, there was a man named Yang Cheng from Huayin. His younger brother was named Yu. The brothers were harmonious and often sat and slept together, and neither of them married. The court summoned Yang Cheng to serve as an Advisor to the Imperial Censorate. He had a strong liking for alcohol and often used a stone from the Yangtze River as a pillow. He would use this stone as collateral for alcohol, sometimes obtaining several hundred liters. When his salary came in, he would immediately redeem the stone. During the reign of Emperor Wenzong of Tang, the Prime Minister Lu Sui was known for his clear and frugal character. He often kept the door closed and did not see guests. His appearance was similar to that of his ancestors, and for this reason, he never looked in a mirror. Additionally, he was deeply affected by his father's death in a foreign land, and he never sat facing west for his entire life; he slept with his head facing west. Emperor Huan Xuan of Jin was known for his greed and corruption, and gold and jade were never far from his hands. Wu Yin's brother Tan Zhi held a funeral and set up a memorial service for his mother. Each time he offered sacrifices, he wept until he fainted. On the seventh sacrifice, he vomited blood and died. Yao Li was extremely thin and weak. When he went out, if the wind was favorable, he could walk; if the wind was against him, he would fall. Emperor Cheng of Han's concubine Zhao Feiyan was very light and could dance on the palm of her hand. Tang's Emperor Gao Kai Dao had an arrow stuck in his head. He had a doctor drill into his skull to remove the arrowhead, and he drank with guests and talked and laughed as usual. During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, the Prime Minister Lou Shide was warm, respectful, and cautious. He never had any minor disputes with anyone. His younger brother was appointed Governor of Dazhou, and on the eve of departure, he warned him: "I love you very much, be careful not to compete with others." His younger brother replied: "If someone spits on your face, wipe it off and leave." Lou Shide said: "This is not acceptable. Those who spit on your face are angry. Wiping it off is against their will. Why not let it dry naturally?" In terms of preserving oneself and avoiding harm, all such cases are like this.

The Cruel King of Cangwu The King of Cangwu was cruel and fond of killing. He would often carry a sword and spear with him, and upon seeing anyone, he would immediately strike and kill them. If he did not kill anyone in a day, he would become sad and unhappy. He Yan often wore women's clothing. Jin Lu Yun, courtesy name Shilong, had his home in Wu, and he had not received any letters from home for a long time. He had a dog named Huang'er. Lu Yun patted the dog's back and said, "Can you deliver a letter to my home for me?" The dog wagged its tail, and Lu Yun placed the letter in a bamboo tube and put it around the dog's neck. Within ten days, the letter reached home, and he received a reply. Chen Zheng served as a high-ranking official in the imperial kitchen. He presented grilled meat, but there was hair through it. Emperor Guangwu ordered Chen Zheng to be beheaded. Chen Zheng said, "I have three crimes, please let me speak them all before I die." He continued, "Charcoal from the mountains cannot burn hair, that is my first crime; a sword kept in a sheath and sharpened daily cannot cut hair, that is my second crime; I and the cook looked at it with six eyes, yet it was not even noticed by the eunuchs' two eyes, that is my third crime." Emperor Guangwu then punished the eunuchs and released Chen Zheng. Emperor Wu of Han traveled from Ganquan to Weiqiao. There was a woman bathing in the Wei River who had breasts that were seven feet long. The emperor found this strange and asked about it. She replied, "The person in the seventh carriage should know me." At that time, Zhang Kuan, an attendant in the imperial carriage, was in the seventh carriage. The emperor sent someone to ask Zhang Kuan, who said, "The ritual for worshipping the stars was not performed with proper solemnity, and that is why this woman appeared."

Shen Yue, a literary figure of the Liang dynasty, had a vast collection of twelve thousand scrolls of family letters, but his heart was perverse. When he heard of someone's goodness, it felt as if a thousand arrows were piercing his heart. Wang Yuanbao, a wealthy merchant of the Tang dynasty, was asked by Emperor Xuanzong about his wealth. He replied, "I beg to tie one piece of silk to one tree on the southern mountain; even when all the trees are tied, my wealth will not be exhausted." People at the time called money "Wang Lao" because of the character "元" (yuan) in his name. Emperor Xuanzong, while on the Hall of Containing the Heavenly Light, looked at the southern mountain and saw a white dragon stretching across the mountain. He asked his attendants if they saw it, and they said no. He then urgently summoned Wang Yuanbao and asked him. Wang Yuanbao said, "I saw a white object stretching across the top of the mountain, but I couldn't determine what it was." The imperial attendants then said, "Why don't we see it, but Wang Yuanbao does?" The emperor replied, "I have heard that extreme wealth rivals extreme nobility. I am the ruler of the empire, and Wang Yuanbao is the wealthiest man in the empire." During Emperor Xuanzong's journey to Sichuan, he reached Mount Dong. The eunuch Gao Lishi pulled the emperor's horse and asked him to dismount. Gao Lishi then bowed four times to the northeast and said, "Your Majesty, you left in haste and did not have the chance to personally bid farewell to the ancestral temples. This mountain is the highest, and from here you can see the region of Qin." Emperor Xuanzong was deeply moved and felt an overwhelming sense of sorrow, and his attendants could not help but weep. Lu Jia, after receiving a large sum of money from Zhao Tuo, the first ruler of Nanyue, divided the wealth among his five sons and instructed them to live separately. However, Lu Jia himself enjoyed leisurely visits to the homes of his five sons, staying for ten days each time and savoring every detail of the service. He also gave his jade swords and jewelry to his sons, and although this was not formal teaching, it was also a wise insight.

During the twilight hours, Bao Zidu of the Wei dynasty was walking in the wilderness when he encountered a scholar who suddenly fell ill with a heartache. Bao Zidu dismounted his horse and massaged the scholar's chest. After a short while, the scholar passed away. Bao Zidu then examined the scholar's bag and found within it a scroll of white paper and ten pieces of gold. He sold two pieces of gold and buried the scholar. The remaining gold was placed under the scholar's neck, and the scroll was laid on his abdomen before Bao Zidu departed. Several years later, as Bao Zidu was traveling, he was chased by a fine horse. Soon after, the owner of the horse recognized it and accused Bao Zidu of theft, asking about the whereabouts of his child. Bao Zidu explained everything. They then accompanied him to the grave, where they found the child, along with eight pieces of gold placed under his neck and the scroll on his abdomen. The entire family went to the authorities to commend Bao Zidu's virtue. As a result, Bao Zidu's reputation grew significantly. Mao Xiaoxian of the Wei dynasty, whose courtesy name was Xiaoxian, served as a high-ranking official known as a Shangshu. No one dared to visit him with gifts of fine clothes and food. Emperor Wu of the Han dynasty remarked, "I am not as capable as Shangshu Mao Xiaoxian in changing the customs of the people." When Dou Ying of the Han dynasty was involved in the campaign against the Seven States, he was granted a thousand pieces of gold. He placed them under the eaves and allowed people to take what they wanted, without bringing them into his private quarters. King Zhao of Song went into exile and told his charioteer, "I know why I have perished." The charioteer asked, "How do you know this?" King Zhao replied, "When I stood dressed in fine clothes, those around me said, 'You are beautiful.' When I spoke and acted, those around me said, 'You are wise.' Neither inside nor outside did I see my faults—how could I not perish?" Realizing his mistakes, he changed his behavior and manner. Three years later, he became renowned for his good deeds in Song, and the people of Song welcomed him back to power, posthumously giving him the title Zhao.

晋王戎，字仲。性鄙吝。家有绿李，子熟时惠人，必钻破其核，恐他人种植之。 王澄出为荆州刺史，送者盈路，见路傍树有一鹊巢，乃自解衣上树，探弄鹊雏，傍若无人。 王右军，永和九年曲水会，用鼠须笔蚕茧纸为《兰亭记叙》，平生之札，最为得意。其后虽书数百本，无一得及者。太宗令御史萧翼密购得之，爵赏之外，别费亿万。太宗临崩，谓高宗曰：“以《兰亭》殉吾，孝也。”遂随梓宫入陵。 汉张骞奉使大月氐，往返一亿三万里，得葡萄、涂林、安石榴，植之于中国。 汉和忆为畿令，常有一虎害人，亿令设槛，得二虎。亿曰：“害人者低头。”一虎低头，亿杀之，其一虎放去。自是猛兽皆出境，吏人以为神君。 后汉郑玄居山东，有疑，莫知所问，遂往入关诣马融。三年不得见。一日融大会，遂见之。登楼问其疑，数十段皆决。语毕，遂归。融谓门人曰：“玄既归，吾道东矣。” 晋王戎，字仲。他生性吝啬。家中有绿李子，当果子熟了的时候分给他人，必定要钻破果核，担心别人会拿去种植。 王澄出任荆州刺史时，送行的人挤满了道路，他看到路边的树上有一个喜鹊巢，便自己脱下衣服爬上树，玩弄喜鹊雏鸟，旁若无人。 王右军在永和九年（353年）的曲水宴会上，用鼠须笔和蚕茧纸书写了《兰亭记叙》，这是他一生中最得意的作品。之后虽然写了数百本，没有一本能及得上原作。太宗命令御史萧翼秘密购得真迹，除了封赏之外，还花费了数亿。太宗临终时对高宗说：“将《兰亭》与我一同埋葬，这是孝道。”于是《兰亭》随太宗的棺椁一同入葬。 汉朝的张骞奉命出使大月氏，往返行程达一亿三万里，带回了葡萄、涂林（一种水果）和安石榴，并在中国种植。 汉朝的和忆担任畿令时，常有一只老虎伤害人，和忆下令设置陷阱，捕获了两只老虎。和忆说：“伤害人的那只会低下头。”其中一只老虎低下了头，和忆杀了它，另一只老虎被放走。从此之后，猛兽都离开了边境，官吏和百姓认为和忆是神明。 后汉的郑玄住在山东，遇到疑难问题，不知道向谁请教，于是前往关中拜访马融。三年未能见到马融。一天马融举行大会，郑玄终于得以相见。郑玄登楼询问自己的疑惑，马融解答了数十个问题。说完后，郑玄便回家了。马融对门人说：“郑玄既然已经归来，我的学问已经传到了东方。”

Jìn Suǒ Mǐ avenged his brother's death by personally killing forty people. When Lǔ Gōngyí Huī served as chancellor, upon returning home, he saw his wife weaving. He then burned the loom and left, telling his wife, "I receive a salary as chancellor, but now you are taking away the people's benefits. Where will the people return to?" Hán Kāngbó lived incognito in a pharmacy, selling goods at a consistent price. Two women came to buy medicine but did not recognize him, so they haggled with him. Kangbó remained firm, and the women said, "How can you be like Kangbó with an unchanged price?" Kangbó then fled and was never seen again. In Gāolí, the king's servant girl stood by the king's side. One day, a cloud descended from the sky, large as an egg, and entered her mouth. She conceived a son after ten months and named him "Dōngmíng," who was skilled in archery. The king feared he would be a threat to the kingdom and wanted to kill him. Dōngmíng fled, drew his bow, and shot at the water. Fish and turtles floated out and formed a bridge, allowing Dōngmíng to cross. Emperor Wèi Wǔ once stayed at the Copper Sparrow Tower. Upon his death, he ordered musicians and dancers to climb the tower and look toward the Western Tomb while dancing and singing. Zhèng Zihua's disciple, Zēng Zhā, liked to collect heron feathers to make hats. Zhèng Bó heard about this and detested it greatly, so he sent a thief to lure and kill him. A gentleman said, "Wearing something inappropriate is a disaster for oneself, as it is not legal or proper attire." The prominent physician Sima Chengju presented a pheasant head fur coat to Emperor Wǔ. The emperor issued an edict saying, "This fur coat is not appropriate to wear and wastes usefulness." He then ordered it to be burned in front of the palace.

Tao Qian, at home, would always filter his wine with the hemp cap on his head whenever it was ready, and after finishing, he would put it back on. During the Jin dynasty, Yang Xiù, courtesy name Zhi Shū, was a wealthy man. In the autumn and winter months, he had his servants carry large jars of wine, and within moments, they would replace them with new jars. In a short while, the wine would be ready. Emperor Wen of Han was frugal. He often collected admonitions and used them to make a screen or curtain. His favored concubine, Shen Fu Ren, did not let her robes touch the ground. During the Jin dynasty, Geng Gǔn, courtesy name Shū Bāo, was frequently warned by his father about drinking. After his father's death, he drank every day without stopping. He then scolded himself, saying, "I have abandoned my father's warnings. How can I teach others?" He took a stick to his grave and beat himself thirty times. When Emperor Ming of Jin was over ten years old, before he became crown prince, Emperor Yuan sat him on his knee and asked, "Which is closer, the sun or Chang'an?" He replied, "The sun is closer." The emperor asked again, "Why do you say the sun is closer?" He replied, "When you look up, you see the sun, but not Chang'an." The emperor was amazed and the next day asked him again in front of his ministers. He replied, "The sun is far, but Chang'an is closer." Emperor Yuan was greatly surprised and asked, "Why is your answer different from yesterday?" He replied, "I have only heard of people coming from Chang'an, but not from the side of the sun." The emperor was even more amazed and immediately made him crown prince. When Guan Lù was seven or eight years old, he played with the children in his neighborhood, drawing the shapes of the sun, moon, and stars on the ground, and his words and actions were unusual. His parents tried to stop him, but he replied, "Even chickens and wild geese know the seasons, how much more so humans!"

Emperor Shun's father, Gushu, listened to the slanderous words of his new wife and often beat Shun. Shun would accept minor punishment but avoid severe punishment, thus preserving his life through filial piety. Huang Ba served as the governor of Yingchuan and summoned officials. While eating in a rural pavilion, birds snatched his food. Huang Ba saw the officials and said, "Are you being snatched by birds?" The officials were startled, thinking Huang Ba knew everything about their actions, and later dared not act improperly. After the fall of Qin, brave men seized gold and jade, but only Ren Shi's son stored grain in granaries. Later, grain cost ten thousand coins per stone, and all gold, jade, and precious goods ended up with Ren Shi. He Shao, courtesy name Jingzu, had a daily food supply costing two thousand coins, and he also enjoyed delicacies from all directions. Even three days of imperial kitchen meals could not match his. The *Lüshi Chunqiu* says: "Using a pearl from the Suo State to shoot at a sparrow thousands of feet high, people laugh at its misuse of value for something trivial." In early Tang, Zhang Gongyi lived with nine generations under one roof. Emperor Gaozong passed by his home during his eastern tour and asked him, "Why is this so?" Zhang Gongyi wrote over a hundred "forbearance" characters and said nothing else. His door was honored and commended. After Emperor Gaozu of Han entered Guanzhong, generals seized jewels and precious goods, but only Xiao He collected the legal codes and regulations of Qin. He became a famous prime minister of Han and was ranked first in merit. Ruan Xiu, courtesy name Xuanzi, lived in poverty and was not married at forty. Wang Dun and others collected money for his marriage, and all were famous scholars. Those who admired him could not even contribute a single coin.

Liu Junliang of the Sui Dynasty lived with his family for generations, and he and his three brothers were united in spirit. At the end of the Great Prosperity era, famine swept across the land. His wife wanted to persuade them to separate, so she secretly took bird chicks from a tree and placed them in different nests, causing the birds to fight. The whole family found this strange. His wife said, "Nowadays, the world is in chaos, and it is a time of war. Even birds cannot coexist, let alone humans." Liu Junliang realized her plan and, at midnight, pulled out her hair and shouted, "This is the traitor who will ruin our family!" He called his brothers and wept, revealing her treachery. They then abandoned her, living in three separate houses but sharing one kitchen. Emperor Zhongzong of Tang was deposed by Empress Wu Zetian and exiled to Fangling. He looked up to heaven and sighed, then threw a stone into the sky, whispering to himself, "If I become emperor, this stone will not fall." The stone was caught by a tree branch and remains there to this day. Another person, while crossing a river to gather firewood, found an ancient mirror and presented it to the emperor. When Zhongzong looked into the mirror, a voice in the reflection said, "If you become emperor, if you become emperor." Within a few days, he ascended to the imperial throne. Xun Fengqian had a deep affection for his wife. In winter, when she was ill with a fever, he went out, sat in the open air, and waited until her body cooled before entering to warm her. His actions were greatly admired by the world. Emperor Xuanzong of Tang visited Shu and arrived at Jibudu in Lizhou, where a white fish swam past the imperial boat. Prince Hejian Xianggong was exceptionally talented and wise. When he first received the order to campaign against Pu Gongyu, there was a vessel on the table that suddenly turned into blood, shocking everyone in the room. No one knew what it meant. Xianggong reflected that he had not wronged any gods or spirits and concluded that this change must be related to Pu Gongyu. People at the time admired his foresight.

Jiang Ziya was enfeoffed to the State of Qi and stayed at an inn. One morning, the innkeeper got up and a man said to him, "The guest slept very soundly, probably not someone who is going to take office." Jiang Ziya jumped up and headed out immediately. Soon, someone came to chase him because he had left the pass, and they stopped him. Duke Huan of Qi and Guan Zhong planned to attack the State of Ju, and the people of Qi knew about it. Duke Huan said to Guan Zhong, "I spoke with my father, and the people know about it, why is that?" Guan Zhong replied, "Perhaps there is a sage among your attendants? Where is the general of the Eastern Capital?" Duke Huan looked around and said, "He is here." Guan Zhong asked, "How do you know it's Ju?" The general replied, "A gentleman has three expressions, so I know." Guan Zhong asked, "What are the three expressions?" The general said, "The expression of joy and celebration, like the sound of bells and drums; the expression of sorrow and mourning, like the sound of mourning music; the expression of sternness and loyalty, like the sound of war." Guan Zhong asked, "How do you know it's Ju?" The general said, "When the king pointed east and south, his mouth was open and his tongue was raised, so I know it's Ju." Zhu Jingze was from Mocheng, Bo Prefecture. He was filial, kind, loyal, and had integrity, unmatched in the world. Six of his family's doorposts were inscribed with official titles, a feat never seen before or since. Gong Yi Hui served as chancellor of the State of Lu. One day, he entered a garden and saw his wife planting melons. He pulled them out and told her, "As a chancellor, competing with the people for profit is not right." He then dismissed her.

Duke Wen of Jin went out to attack Wei, and the marquis looked up and laughed. The duke asked, "Why are you laughing?" The marquis replied, "I am laughing at my neighbor. My neighbor had a wife who was going to her private home. On the way, he met a mulberry woman and was pleased with her, speaking to her; but when he looked back at his wife, he saw that she too was being called. That is why I secretly laughed at him." The duke understood his words and stopped, leading his troops back. Before they arrived, there was an attack on the northern border. Once upon a time, there was a man by the sea who daily played with seagulls, bringing hundreds of them along. His father said, "I heard that seagulls follow you, so you can bring them along. I want to play with them." The next day, the man went, and the seagulls flew around but did not land. Because the birds sensed his intention and were afraid. Yao Hong surrendered his wife and children to Liu Jiu, who executed him in the market of Jiankang. Within a hundred miles, the grass all withered and died. Fu Jian entrusted his government to Wang Meng, with no doubt in matters big or small. When Wang Meng died, his son Pi plotted a rebellion. Fu Jian said, "The Chancellor at his deathbed, left ten oxen for the fields, but did not hear him ask for a position for his son. No one knows a son better than his father; how true these words are." He pardoned him instead of executing him. During the Yifeng period of the Tang dynasty, the Shangshu Lang Ouyang Tong was reinstated as judge of the Hall. Every time he went to court, he would walk barefoot to the city gate, then put on his shoes. When he reached the office, he would sit on the ground and use straw as a mat, speaking only about official matters and never opening his mouth otherwise. When he returned home, he would always wear mourning clothes and wail without a fixed time. The court took pity on him and allowed him to continue his mourning duties.

Emperor Xuan of Han had hair on his feet, and wherever he lived, there was always a radiant glow. Yan Han of the Jin dynasty was known for his filial piety. His brother, who had taken too many medicines, died at home. Yan Han then opened the coffin and revived him. His mother, wife, and family members were all exhausted, but Yan Han completely withdrew from social affairs, serving his brother for thirteen years without ever showing signs of fatigue. Song Yan, who served as chancellor of Qi, was dismissed and returned home. He summoned the gatekeeper Tien Rao and twenty-two others, saying, "Are there any of you gentlemen who can help me join another lord?" Tien Rao and the others all remained silent and did not respond. Song Yan said, "Alas! Scholars and officials are easy to find but hard to use." Tien Rao replied, "It is not that scholars and officials are easy to find but hard to use; it is that you cannot use them. If you cannot use them, you will have an unfair heart, which means you are first examining yourself before criticizing others." Song Yan asked, "What does it mean to first examine oneself before criticizing others?" Tien Rao said, "Three dou of grain is not enough for scholars, but you have more than enough for your geese and ducks. The fruits and nuts from your garden are thrown around by the women in your harem, but scholars do not get a single taste. The luxurious silk and brocade are worn by your concubines, but scholars and officials do not get them as their salary. Wealth is something you take lightly, but death is something scholars take seriously. You cannot give up what you take lightly and make scholars achieve what they value. It is like having a dull knife stored away while expecting a sharp one to be used—how difficult is that!" Song Yan felt ashamed and left his seat, saying, "I have made a mistake."

The King of Chu invited Zhuangzi, who replied, "I have heard that a divine turtle, after dying for three years, was placed on a mat and then stored in the ancestral temple. Is it better to die with honor or to live and drag one's tail in the mud?" Thus, he did not go to Chu for the invitation. Ji An was not happy to be the governor of Huaiyang and firmly refused the position. The emperor said, "You can govern while lying down." In the subsequent chapter, When Zhao Jianzi died but had not yet been buried, Zhongmou rebelled. After five days, Xiangzi raised an army to attack them. Before the siege was fully encircled, ten zhang of the city wall collapsed on its own. Xiangzi ordered the army to retreat. The officials advised, "Your lord has committed a grave sin, yet the city wall collapsed on its own. Isn't this a sign of divine assistance? Why should we retreat?" Xiangzi replied, "I heard from Shuxiang that a gentleman does not take advantage of another's peril, nor does he exploit another's danger. Let them repair the city wall before we attack." Zhongmou heard this and surrendered.

Emperor Taikang hunted in the outskirts of Luoyang for ten days without returning. His five younger brothers carried their mother to join him. Ultimately, he lost his kingdom. Emperor Gaozu of Han wanted to replace the crown prince with Prince Zhao. Empress Lu asked Zhang Liang for advice. Zhang Liang said, "There are four worthies in the South Mountain who lived in seclusion and did not serve Qin. The crown prince should humbly invite them. If the four elders come, they will support the crown prince, which will be a great help." Thus, Dongyuan Gong, Xialuangong, Jueli Master, and Qiliji all accompanied the crown prince to pay homage. Emperor Gaozu said, "I have gained the empire, but I have not been here. Now they are following my son, why?" The four elders said, "Your Majesty is disrespectful, and we are ashamed to come. The current crown prince is wise and virtuous, so we support him." Thus, the crown prince was secured. Emperor Gaozu told Consort Qi, "The wings are already formed, it is difficult to shake them." Zhang Jia served as the governor of Xiangzhou and inquired about past practices when he arrived in the capital. It was said that past and present governors mostly did not live outside the city walls, unless they died in exile, then they were demoted. Zhang Jia examined the records and found that starting from the death of Wei Zhi Jiong in service to the state during the Northern Zhou dynasty. He then built a temple for Wei Zhi Jiong and worshiped him four times a year. Three years later, he was appointed as the Grand Commandant of the Capital Garrison. When he reached Wu Jing, he was bestowed a ceremonial robe and hat, and later all received promotions.

During the Han Dynasty, Geng Shouchang established the granary system, buying grain at low prices when it was cheap and reducing prices when it was expensive, showing concern for the people and thus setting an example for the state. During the Tang Dynasty, Cui Qun, a prime minister, was known for his integrity and prestige. In the Yuanhe period, he served as the imperial examiner in charge of the spring examinations. After his term ended, his wife, Lady Li, often advised him to establish estates as a provision for their descendants. Cui Qun smiled and replied, "I have thirty beautiful estates and good farmland spread throughout the land. What more could you possibly worry about?" Lady Li said, "I have not heard of such estates belonging to you." Cui Qun responded, "Last year, I selected thirty candidates for the spring examination. Are they not like good farmland?" Lady Li said, "If that is the case, you are not a student of the former prime minister Lu? But in the past, when you held the power of literary selection, you had someone tell his son to avoid the spring examination. If you consider these candidates as your estates, then the Lu family's estate is now barren." Cui Qun felt ashamed and withdrew, not eating for several days. Wei Yuanzhong of the Tang Dynasty had a noble and upright spirit. Initially serving as the governor of Luoyang, he was sentenced to execution in the marketplace. After he was seated for execution, Empress Wu Zetian, recognizing his merit in suppressing Xu Jingye's rebellion, specially ordered his release. The person carrying out the imperial decree hurriedly announced the release of Wei Yuanzhong. The announcement arrived first, and the executioner helped him stand up. Wei Yuanzhong said, "The imperial decree has not arrived; how can I seek to live?" After a while, the decree finally arrived. The onlookers were all impressed by his calm and extraordinary demeanor.

Emperor Yang of Sui was tyrannical, and Du Fuwei rebelled in Qi Prefecture. Emperor Yang sent Chen Leng to attack him. Chen Leng's subordinate shot Du Fuwei in the forehead. Du Fuwei, enraged, said, "I will not remove this arrow until I kill the one who shot me." He then charged in with great effort, captured the shooter, ordered the arrow to be removed, and then beheaded the man, carrying his head into Chen Leng's camp, which led to Chen Leng's defeat. During the reign of Emperor Hui of Han, Shu Sun Tong indirectly advised the emperor, saying, "In ancient times, there were offerings in spring. Now that cherries are ripe, may Your Majesty pick them." The practice of offering fruits began from this event. Emperor Gao Yang of Northern Qi was cruel and violent. Imperial consort Xue committed a minor fault, and he immediately killed and dismembered her. He held her leg and played the pipa on it, then lamented, "A beautiful woman is hard to find again." Li Tongjie, the governor of Cangjing, rebelled during the Tang dynasty. Wang Zhixing led troops from Xu and Si to attack in Di Prefecture. At the time, Li Tongjie sent a skilled speaker dressed in a short robe to sit on the city wall and curse Wang Zhixing. The military officials were ashamed, and Wang Zhixing covered his ears and could not bear to hear it. One soldier said, "This can be used to throw a stone to hit his head." Wang Zhixing was pleased and said, "If it hits, I will reward you with ten thousand gold coins." He prepared to throw a stone, which hit his head and fell off with the stone. The army cheered, and the city walls shook.

The Han official Palace had a reputation among the Xiongnu, who wished to meet him. They sent envoys to request an audience with Palace. Palace reported to the emperor, saying, "People in the outer regions value grand and imposing figures. I am unattractive, so it would be better to select someone magnificent to represent me." The emperor then sent the Grand Commandant Wei Ying to meet the Xiongnu. During the Eastern Han dynasty, eunuchs Zhang Rang and Zhao Zhong held the reins of power, elevating butchers and wine sellers to positions of honor. Emperor Ling told those around him, "Zhang the Attendant is my father, and Zhao the Attendant is my mother." Thus, it was these two eunuchs, Zhao and Zhang, who ultimately brought about the downfall of the Han dynasty. Zhang Anshi would always recommend virtuous and capable individuals without letting them know. Sometimes, people would come to his door to express their gratitude, but he would not receive them. He lamented throughout his life, saying, "How can one receive an official appointment in public and express thanks privately?" The Yu family was a wealthy clan in the state of Liang. They built a tall tower overlooking a major road, where they held nightly singing, banquets, and games. When a player won, they would cover their mouth and laugh. One day, three guests passed by the tower, and a kite carrying a dead rat fell on one of the guests' faces, landing on the person who was laughing. The two guests who saw this plotted together, saying, "The Yu family has been rich and happy for a long time. We have not trespassed on their wealth, so why should we be humiliated?" They then gathered a crowd to destroy the Yu family. The saying goes, "The disaster of pride and extravagance is not singular in its cause." Bing Dan Manrong of the Han dynasty cultivated his aspirations and distanced himself from power, holding a position of 600 stones and then resigning, fearing power and thus bringing disaster upon himself. Zhang Heng died, and Puyang was born; at the time, people considered Puyang to be the successor of Zhang Heng.

In the early Tang dynasty, there was a man named Kan Leng who was skilled in using a long sword. The sword was over ten feet long, and each time he drew it, he killed several people. No one succeeded him in his skill. Bo Zhao was the maternal uncle of Emperor Wen of Han. Because he killed Han envoys, Emperor Wen could not bring himself to enforce the law and ordered the court officials to wear mourning clothes and mourn for him. Bo Zhao then committed suicide. Some say that at a drinking party, Bo Zhao poured wine that was not full, and a junior official criticized him. After the incident, Bo Zhao had the official killed. When the emperor heard about it, he became angry, and thus had this action taken. In the north, there were the Xiongnu, whose appearance was like humans, but whose feet were like horse hooves. They were called "horse-footed Tujue." Diu Tao was on long-term military service, and his wife Su was skilled in poetry. She wove a poem in a circular pattern to express the feelings of separation and distance. When read in any direction, it all made sense. Jia Da was five years old, and his sister held him while he listened to the neighbors reading. When he grew up, both of them were able to understand the classics. His sister asked, "I never taught you, how did you learn this?" He replied, "My sister held me while I listened to the neighbors reading, and I understood everything." When he grew up, he read more widely. He became famous throughout the land, people brought grain and silk to study under him, and his family became very wealthy. People of the time believed that Jia Da was "tilling with his tongue." Zhong Yao married his principal wife at the age of seventy. Wei Qing, originally a charioteer for Princess Pingyang, was chosen to marry her after the death of Prince Cao Shou, as he was the most prestigious person in the country. When the princess asked, "He was my charioteer, what should I do?" the messenger replied, "There is no one in the world like General Wei Qing." So, he married Princess Pingyang.

The wet nurse of Emperor Wu of Han relied on her favor, and her family acted recklessly. The emperor was angry and exiled the wet nurse to the border, where she came to bid farewell to him. Guo She-ren said to her, "When you leave now, just look back at me several times, and I will save you from being exiled." The wet nurse followed his advice and looked back at him. Guo She-ren then scolded, "Old woman, why did you look back? The emperor is now strong, did he really need your milk?" The emperor was pleased and decided not to exile the wet nurse. In the Qin dynasty, You Zhan served Emperor Qin Shi Huang, standing on the hall. The laws of Qin were strict, and without an imperial order, one could not move. During heavy rain, the guards stood under their shields in the courtyard. You Zhan wanted to help them, so he joked, "Men with shields, you are tall but standing in the rain; I am short but standing on the hall." Emperor Qin Shi Huang heard this and ordered them to move to the eaves. When Han Gaozu was in humble circumstances, he often visited his sister-in-law with guests for a meal. His sister-in-law was annoyed by him and pretended the soup was finished, worrying about her pot. Han Gaozu resented her. After becoming emperor, he bestowed a title on his son as the Lord of Worrying Soup. When someone asked the emperor about this, he replied, "Her mother was not a virtuous woman." During the reign of Duke Jing of Qi, a man was convicted of a crime, and the duke was angry, ordering him to be dismembered, saying, "Anyone who dares to remonstrate will be executed!" Yanzi held the man's head in his left hand and the knife in his right, asking the duke, "Since ancient times, when did sage rulers begin to dismember people?" Duke Jing immediately stopped the execution and said, "The fault lies with me." Emperor Yuan of Liang was blind in one eye and favored Lady Xu. Lady Xu was jealous, and later resented the emperor. Whenever he summoned her, she would only half-dress herself to see him, implying that since the emperor had only one eye, he was not fully whole. The emperor personally killed her.

In the village of Shi, there was an old man named Zhu Ji, skilled at raising a flock of chickens, all of which had names. He sold chicken eggs and earned millions. One day, he fled into the mountains, and no one knew where he went. Li Huiguang, having rebelled in Pu, was punished by the court according to the law. He had seven sons, and his eldest was named . He told his younger brothers, "We brothers cannot die at the hands of soldiers, so we did not choose to take our own lives!" So they took up swords and cut off their brothers' heads, piling them up and placing the sword in the middle. They used their hearts to temper the sword, and it pierced through their chests. Those who heard about it were saddened. In the city of Guancheng in Zheng, there lived a man named Zheng Qianzhang. He was a disreputable person who often drank in taverns, over fifty years old, and unknown to anyone. He would return drunk and sleep in the guest quarters. At night, he reached out to take a wine vessel, and was suddenly pulled into a pit by a ghost. He struggled and called out in panic. His relatives lit candles and came to help, but they could not control the situation. He gradually sank, until his chest and head were in the ground, and suddenly his entire body was submerged. They gathered the crowd and dug with shovels, reaching a depth of over a meter, and found a dried bone about eight or nine inches long. They searched again, but found nothing else. They buried it. Wang E was a subordinate officer under Xin Jingguo. Xin was the governor of Changsha and greatly underestimated him. One day, they were playing ball, and the competition was intense. Wang lifted his head and exhaled, and his breath rose several meters high, like a white silk banner. Xin told his wife, "This is a sign of great fortune." So he married his younger sister to him. Wang eventually became a general and a high-ranking official.

A man from the far west presented a string-repairing glue to the emperor. The emperor did not believe it, so he cut it and joined it, then had someone pull it. When it broke elsewhere, the glued part remained as before. In the state of Wei, there was a woman who was extremely beautiful but remained unmarried beyond the usual time because she had a chronic wound on her right knee, which was swollen and oozing pus. When Hua Tuo passed by, her father asked him about it. Hua Tuo said, "Have someone ride a horse, tie a chestnut-colored dog to it, and walk thirty miles. Upon returning, cut off the dog's right foot and hang it." Soon after, a red snake emerged from the wound and entered the dog's foot, and her illness was cured. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, there was a man named Mr. Zhang Guolao, whose age was unknown, and he came from Xingzhou. The emperor welcomed him into the palace and showed him great respect. He asked him anything, and Mr. Guolao knew the answers to all questions. One day, a Daoist named Ye Jingneng, who also had much knowledge, asked the emperor, "Who is Mr. Guolao?" Ye Jingneng replied, "I know him. But if I speak, I will die. I dare not speak. If Your Majesty removes your hat and shoes and commands me, I can live." The emperor agreed. Ye Jingneng said, "This is the white bat spirit from the beginning of chaos." After he finished speaking, blood flowed from his seven orifices, and he fell to the ground. The emperor rushed to him, and Mr. Guolao calmly said, "This child talks too much. If not punished, it will ruin the affairs of the world." The emperor pleaded with him for a long time, and Mr. Guolao wiped his face with water, and he revived. Later, Mr. Guolao returned to his place of seclusion in Xingzhou, and suddenly disappeared.

Liu Yan of the Eastern Han Dynasty, styled Junqing, served as the Grand Master of Ceremonies during the reign of Emperor Ling. Observing the turmoil in the imperial family, he intended to seek refuge in Jiaozhou to avoid disaster. The imperial censor Dong Fu told Liu Yan, "I have often seen that Yizhou has the aura of a sovereign." Liu Yan then sought to take control of Yizhou and was subsequently appointed. Upon arriving in Shu, he thought about what Dong Fu had said and created imperial carriages and flags, following exactly the standards of a king. Suddenly one day, a heavenly fire descended and burned, destroying all the things he had created. Liu Yan died of a boil on his back. During the reign of Qin Shi Huang, a sudden flood in Chang'an County threatened to inundate the entire county. The county clerk Quan Gan entered to report to the magistrate, who said, "Today, why are you acting like a fish?" Quan Gan replied, "You are also acting like a fish head." After speaking, the area sank and turned into a lake. Wang Ya, a Tang Dynasty chancellor, was extremely extravagant and luxurious. He dug a large well in his courtyard, made a wooden cabinet, and secured it tightly. He threw jewels, pearls, and precious stones from all over the world into the water, drawing water to supply his drinking. Shortly after, he committed a crime and was executed by divine forces, with his family being wiped out. His bones and flesh turned golden in color.

**The *Shen Yi Ji* notes:** In the remote wilderness to the southwest, there is a beast that resembles a rabbit, has a human face, and can speak. Its heart is always deceitful, and it often lies, saying "east" when it means "west" and "south" when it means "north." Its name is "Gui." **During the reign of Sun Quan of Wu, he was hunting in Wuchang when a divine woman appeared and said, "Today you will hunt a strange beast." She then disappeared. Shortly after, a leopard was caught. The woman reappeared and said, "Set up its tail at my place and establish a shrine there."** Some say that the establishment of a shrine with a tiger's tail began with Sun Quan. **The *Thirty Kingdoms Spring and Autumn* records:** During the reign of Zhang Zhonghua of the Western Jin, he intended to eliminate Zhang Zu of Xihé. Suddenly, dozens of horses in Zhang Zu's stable lost their tails. Shortly after, Zhang Zu met his demise. **During the Tang Dynasty, Jia Zhiyan, a loyal minister of Emperor Dezong, was exiled to the South Sea because his father had leaked confidential matters. The emperor was furious and ordered Jia Zhiyan to be executed with poison. Jia Zhiyan approached the imperial messenger and asked to take the cup himself and drink it for his father. The messenger agreed. Jia Zhiyan then held the cup and drank it, dying immediately. The wine came out from his left foot, and he revived. He reported this to the emperor, and his father was subsequently exiled to the South Sea. Later, Jia Zhiyan returned to Dongping after receiving imperial favor. He became renowned for his integrity and was appointed as a *Jianyi Daifu* (Adviser to the Emperor). Jia Zhiyan's wife, Dong, was also a woman of extraordinary virtue. When Jia Zhiyan was exiled with his father, he told Dong, "Death may come at any time, and we may not meet again." He instructed her to remarry. Dong entered her room, tied her hair with a rope, took a brush, and had Jia Zhiyan seal the note, writing, "This will not be opened unless by you, and it will remain sealed until death." Twenty-two years later, they reunited, and the old note was still intact. When they washed their hair with oil, her hair fell out.

After the court session, Emperor Taizong returned home in anger and said, "I must eventually kill this country bumpkin!" The Empress Wenxuan asked, "Who has the Great One become angry with?" The emperor replied, "It's just that old soldier Wei Zheng, who humiliated me in front of everyone." The empress then entered the palace, dressed in a ceremonial robe, and bowed down in the hall. The emperor was surprised and asked, "Why did you do that?" The empress said, "I have heard that when the ruler is wise, the ministers are loyal. Wei Zheng can speak frankly; it is because the Great One is wise that there are loyal ministers. I dare to congratulate you." The emperor was greatly pleased and valued Wei Zheng even more. Cao Cao secretly spoke to one of his attendants, saying, "You may carry a blade into my room tomorrow. I will have someone seize you; do not speak, and I will reward you greatly." The person did not understand and remained silent until he died. Cao Cao used this to deceive the people, demonstrating his ability to observe the subtlest expressions of others. When Zilu went out, he saved Lu, caused chaos in Qi, destroyed Wu, strengthened Jin, and made Yue dominant. During the Tang dynasty, Zhang Ji passed the imperial examinations seven times. Yuan Banqian commented, "Zhang's writing is like green coins, a million chosen, a million hit." At that time, people referred to Zhang as the "Green Coin Scholar."

During the Tang Dynasty, Di Renjie served as the Director of the Dali Temple, where he submitted his annual performance evaluation to the Ministry of Personnel, only to have it rejected. When asked, "What achievements do you have?" the temple responded, "He handles 12,000 criminal cases each year." The Ministry of Personnel then specially promoted him to a higher rank. Emperor Gao Yang of the Northern Qi was known for his cruelty. His younger brother, Prince Nanyang, suggested that they collect a group of scorpions and place them in a large container, then strip a person naked and let them be stung by the scorpions. The person cried out in pain, unable to endure the suffering. Emperor Gao Yang laughed and sent a letter to his brother, saying, "How could you not tell me about this delightful event earlier?" Emperor Xuanzong of Tang held a ceremony at the Qinzheng Pavilion, where various performances were held. He seated An Lushan to the east to watch. The Crown Prince advised, "Throughout history, there has never been a minister sitting beside the emperor to watch performances." The emperor replied, "He has an unusual appearance, so I am protecting him with rituals." On another occasion, during a night banquet, An Lushan became drunk and transformed into a creature with a pig's body and a dragon's head. The attendants quickly reported this to the emperor, who said, "He has a dragon's head and a pig's body; he cannot do anything." Despite this, the emperor did not kill him, and An Lushan eventually caused chaos in China. The River Master once lived by the river, unknown by his name or surname, with no desires for worldly affairs and never stepping into a city. Emperor Wen of Han summoned him, but he did not come. The emperor then went to see him. The River Master claimed to have thoroughly studied the *Laozi* and was greatly despised by the world. When the emperor spoke to him, he said, "I am your sovereign; you are my minister. Do you not have respect for your sovereign?" The River Master then leaped into the air and said, "I am not in the heavens above, nor in the earth below. Your majesty may be respected, but what can you do to me?" The emperor apologized, and the River Master descended. They discussed the meaning of the *Laozi*, but the emperor could not convince him.

Lu Yun had a penchant for laughter. Once, he visited the Grand Works Minister Zhang Hua, who had a lot of facial hair, which Zhang Hua kept in a bag. When Lu Yun saw Zhang Hua, he laughed so hard that he fell over before he could even bow. Another time, while he was on a boat, he saw his reflection in the water and laughed so much that he fell into the water, nearly drowning to death. Emperor Taizong considered Wei Zheng as his mirror, telling those around him: "By using the past as a mirror, we can see success and failure; by using bronze as a mirror, we can know beauty and ugliness; by using people as a mirror, we can know good and evil. I use these three mirrors to distinguish prosperity and decline. Now that Wei Zheng has died, I have lost one of my mirrors." Li Heng planted a thousand orange trees in Jingling, reaping their benefits every year. He told his son: "I have a thousand 'wooden slaves,' which can serve as your livelihood, providing you with food and clothing for life." Ji Tong served as the governor of Liaodong for thirty years. The emperor praised his achievements and granted him a million coins. After quelling the rebellion of the Seven States, Zhou Yafu returned but was not given a position of trust, leaving him unhappy and discontented. The emperor noticed this and, during a banquet, gave him meat without any bones. Zhou Yafu showed signs of resentment. The emperor then handed him over to the Imperial Censor. Zhou Yafu starved for ninety days on straw mats, eventually dying suddenly. It was predicted beforehand that he would die of starvation due to his physical condition, and this prediction came true. Lu Meng, at the age of sixteen, followed his brother-in-law Deng Dang to fight against bandits. He shouted and charged forward, and Deng Dang could not stop him. Upon returning home, he told his mother: "Who can live in poverty? With merit, one can achieve wealth and honor." He also said: "If one does not venture into the tiger's den, how can one catch the tiger's cub?" Thus, he became famous. During Emperor Wen of Sui's reign, he had already become tyrannical. Ships measuring three zhang in length were deemed too large and capable of hiding rebels, so they were confiscated by the government.

Once upon a time, a man was out traveling when he encountered a rabbit running along and hitting a tree, which caused it to die. He took the rabbit. Later, he neglected his family and livelihood, sitting daily beneath the tree, waiting for the rabbit to reappear. Someone who passed by saw him and asked, "Why are you waiting for the rabbit like this?" During the Later Han Dynasty, when Dou Wu was born, his mother also gave birth to a snake, which was then sent to the mountains. When Dou Wu's mother died, a large snake appeared beside her coffin, bowing repeatedly with blood flowing from its mouth before departing. Zhi Du served as the governor of Jinan and executed more than 300 families of the powerful clans, without regard for his own family. During the Later Han Dynasty, Wei Cheng's mother, Zong Shi, extensively studied the classics and established a school with 120 students, teaching them through a gauze window. Xun Song had a daughter named Guan. When Xun Song was the governor of Xiangyang, he was besieged by Du Lu. Xun Song sought help from General Shi Lian, the Southern Pacification Commander. At the age of 13, Guan led more than a thousand brave warriors, scaled the city walls to break through the siege, and fought their way forward to seek aid from Shi Lian. Shi Lian lent her troops to rescue Xun Song, and the bandits fled in disarray. Qidu Helì, a chieftain and governor of the Western tribes, was granted the title of Right Guard General and established many military achievements early in his career. When he was sent to campaign against the Liao, he reached Baique City, where he was surrounded by the enemy. He suffered a severe wound from an enemy spear in his waist. The emperor ordered that, upon capturing the city, anyone who wielded a spear should be brought to him. Emperor Taizong handed over a man named Gao Tu Bochu, ordering Helì to kill him. Helì said, "Even dogs and horses remain loyal to their masters; how much more so humans? He was acting on behalf of his master, using a spear to stab me, which shows his bravery. We have never met, so how could he be my enemy?" Thus, he spared him.

Xie Chao possessed a rare and exceptional demeanor. Whenever there were humble and newly emerging individuals, he exerted effort in promoting them. On the day of his death, over four thousand people wrote eulogies for him. Lu Su prioritized helping those in urgent need with a sense of righteousness. When Zhou Yu was serving as the magistrate of Juchao and was in mourning for his mother, he passed by Lu Su seeking grain. At that time, Lu Su had two piles of grain, each containing three thousand shi. He pointed to one pile and gave it to Zhou Yu. Zhou Yu was impressed by this and thus established a friendship with him. Lu Su eventually became the dominant figure in Wu. The wife of Jin's Prince Wenhun, Lady Zhong, whose name was Yan, was known for her discernment. Her daughter, named Shu, was seeking a husband. There was a son of a military official who was exceptionally talented, and she wished to marry him. She instructed him to interact with a group of officials, while she secretly observed him from behind a curtain. She said, "This person has the potential to stand out, but his circumstances are poor, and his life is short, which will not allow him to fully display his abilities." She then stopped the marriage. That man died a few months later. Yi Yin carried a tripod to seek an audience with King Tang. King Tang ordered him to prepare a dish, which turned out to be very delicious. Impressed, King Tang granted him an audience. When asked, Yi Yin replied, "If I were to govern the country, I would do so in the same manner." He was then appointed as chancellor and successfully established the Way of the King. Emperor Xuanzong of Tang visited Shu, and Pei Shiyuan followed the emperor. On the way, they compared the current high officials. Pei Shiyuan asked, "How is Yao Yuanzhi?" The emperor replied, "He is both talented and vigorous." He then asked, "How is Song Qi?" The emperor replied, "He is virtuous but stubborn." They discussed several others, and the emperor provided fitting evaluations for each. Finally, Pei Shiyuan asked, "How is Li Linfu?" The emperor replied, "He envies the talented and fears the capable, unparalleled in ancient and modern times." Pei Shiyuan said, "If the emperor knew this, why would he entrust him so deeply?" The emperor lowered his head and remained silent.

During the Jin dynasty, Du Yu was appointed as the governor of Jing Province. All the guests came to congratulate him, but Yang Hu and Pei Kai arrived later. They sat together on the same bed, and Yang Hu became angry, saying, "Du Yu treats his guests with such intimacy." He then brushed off his clothes and left. During the reign of Emperor Wuzong, the Chancellor Li Deyu was extremely extravagant. Each time he ate a bowl of soup, it cost about thirty thousand coins, made from a mixture of precious stones, pearls, jade, cinnabar, and realgar. After three boils, he would discard the residue into the gutter. Emperor Zhao of Han took the granddaughter of Shangguan Jie as his wife. Her father was named An, and she was made empress at the age of six. During the Kaiyuan period, Xiao Yingshi, at the age of nineteen, was selected as a Jinshi (imperial scholar). He was well-versed in Confucianism, Buddhism, and Taoism, but he had a narrow and hasty temper, and was extremely irritable. He often employed a hired servant named Du Liang. Every time he disciplined him, it would result in injuries. After healing, he continued to serve as usual. Someone advised Du Liang, "You are a hired servant. Why don't you find a good master instead of suffering like this?" Du Liang replied, "I am not stupid; I only admire his talent and his extensive knowledge." He was so attached that he eventually died.

In the state of Wu, Zhuge Ke's wife woke up early and sat in front of her makeup table. A servant stood behind her, when suddenly she jumped up and hit the beam, opening her eyes wide and shouting loudly, saying, "The household is in danger." A short while later, the family members returned and said, "Ke was killed by Sun Jun." Chen Zhonggong, in his work "Yiwen Ji," recorded the following: "Zhang Guangding, who encountered chaos and had to flee, found a four-year-old girl who could not walk and was unwilling to abandon her. He placed her in a basket and hung it in an ancient tomb, intending to retrieve bones in the future. After three years, he returned to retrieve the girl and found that she was still alive. When he asked her, she replied, 'When food ran out, I became hungry. I saw something nearby, extended my neck to breathe, and imitated it, so I survived.' Zhang Guangding entered the tomb to see the object and found that it was a turtle. Chen Zhonggong's words are undoubtedly true." Emperor Xuanzong happened to play a game of Go with Prince Ning and summoned Consort Yang to stand and watch. Suddenly, the wind blew over Consort Yang's veil, covering the headgear of the musician He Huizhi. The fragrance was strong and lingering. After the emperor's journey to Shu, he returned, and He Huizhi presented the headgear to the emperor. The emperor held it and wept, saying, "This was the time when a Western country presented three pieces of incense, which were given to Consort Yang and called 'Rui Longnao.'"

The daughter of Xunshuang of the Jin dynasty married Yin Yu. After a year, Yin Yu died. Xunshuang, considering his daughter's high talent and spirited nature, pitied her for being young and widowed, and wished to change her mind and have her remarry Guo Yi. He sent his close relatives to ask her. The woman secretly carried a knife with her, and Xunshuang took it from her. Later, he gathered many relatives and set up a grand banquet. Just as they were about to start, he ordered Guo Yi to suddenly appear before her. The woman ordered candles to be placed at the four corners and met Guo Yi. Guo Yi only sat upright. Xunshuang immediately ordered a bath to be prepared. The woman sent two maidservants out of the house to fetch other things, and then she pricked her arm and wrote with her blood on a fan, saying, "Return my body to the Yin family." She hanged herself and died. In ancient times, there was a man named Foolish Old Man who lived in the shadow of the mountains, and his entrance and exit were blocked. He encouraged his descendants to move the mountains. The mountain god said, "The mountain is extremely high. How can you move it?" The old man replied, "I have children, and they will have grandchildren. Generation after generation, we swear to move it. Why not?" Thus, the god commanded the Qu'e clan to move the mountains. During the Qin dynasty, 240 scholars were buried alive at Lishan. The *Shi* and *Shu* (Books) were burned, all according to Li Si's plan, to make the common people ignorant. Helian Bobo, originally known as Qushizi, changed his surname to Helian Bobo, saying, "bright and connected with heaven." He killed people, piling their heads into a *jingguan* (trophy heap), calling it the Skull Tower. He boiled clay to build a city. If a spearhead penetrated, the builder was beaten to death; if it did not penetrate, the spearhead maker was beaten to death. In making weapons, if an arrow penetrated armor, the armor maker was killed; if it did not penetrate, the bow maker was killed. No one knew what to do.

Liang Zhou Xingsi served as a Scattered Rider and Attendant, intelligent and full of talent and thought. Emperor Wu produced a thousand words without chapters or sentences, ordered Zhou Xingsi to arrange them, and thus created the Thousand Character Classic. Upon returning, both his eyes were lost. Upon death, upon opening it, the heart was like scooping dry mud. Liu Youqiu, from the position of magistrate of Chaoyi, became a Secretary of the Central Secretariat and was appointed chancellor within three days. Emperor Taizong said that Yu Shiniu has five excellences: the first is broad knowledge, the second is virtue and conduct, the third is calligraphy and writing, the fourth is rhetoric and style, the fifth is loyalty and integrity. Had his image painted in the Hall of Bright Clouds, lived to the age of eighty-one and died. After killing Liu Yu, then saw those who were superior to him and killed them all. Men aged seven were not allowed to enter the city. To convey, all used women, ordered those with strong voices to proclaim his commands. In dire straits, then first hanged his wife, children, and sisters, then threw himself into the fire and died. Cao Cao was cruel to human life but valued skills. There was a singing boy who was very intelligent and whose voice could reach the clouds. Cao Cao loved his voice and did not dare to kill him, then sought out two women among his concubines whose voices were like his, secretly ordered them to teach him, and after several months they succeeded. After hearing it, immediately killed the previous ones. Fan Can, style name Chengming. When Prince Fang of Qi was deposed, Chengming wept bitterly and therefore did not take office. When Sima Shi assisted in governance, when summoned did not respond, claimed illness, pretended madness and did not speak, feet did not touch the ground, people did not hear his voice. When there were marriages in the family, consulted him, if they agreed, his expression did not change; if they disagreed, he would not sleep peacefully. Family members gauged his wishes from the carriage where he slept.

During the Tang Dynasty, Di Renjie served as a pacification commissioner and removed 1,200 superfluous shrines. Zhang Xun, while defending Ningling, was in a desperate situation and felt isolated. Every time he shouted, geese flew in rows against him. Ruan Ji, during his mother's mourning period, cried so intensely that he vomited blood and became emaciated, standing like a skeleton. Shi Miao, while serving as the magistrate of Shouchun, visited Jiang Ji, the governor of the region, but Jiang was drunk and did not receive him. Upon returning home, Shi carved a figure out of wood, wrote "drunkard Jiang Ji" on it, and shot it with an arrow. The local officials heard about this and could not control him. Duke Ji of Lu, as chancellor of the state of Lu, had no concubines dressed in silk and no horses fed on grain, emphasizing his frugality and moral integrity. The Ailao tribe, whose ancestors were a woman named Sha Yi who lived in Ailao Mountain, caught fish in the water and was touched by a submerged log, which led to her pregnancy. She gave birth to ten sons after ten months. The present-day southwestern barbarians are her descendants. Emperor Gaozu of Tang raised an army in Bingzhou, and his sister-in-law, Lady Chai, gathered troops in the regions of Jie and Du to support him. After Emperor Gaozu ascended the throne, he bestowed the title of Princess of Pingyang on her, known as the "Lady General," and she achieved notable military accomplishments. She was the first woman to receive a fiefdom without being a wife of a noble and was buried with a full ceremonial escort, setting a precedent. Duke Wen of Wei asked Gu Jianzi, "If one's father, son, brother, and minister are all virtuous, can one rely on them?" Gu replied, "Even if one's father is as virtuous as Yao, his son Dan Zhu was arrogant; even if one's son is as virtuous as Shun, his father Gushu was imprisoned; even if one's brother is as virtuous as Shun, his younger brother Xiang was arrogant; even if one's younger brother is as virtuous as Dan, his elder brother Guan was executed; even if one's minister is as virtuous as Tang and Wu, they were overthrown by Jie and Zhou. Can one rely on virtue alone to govern the state?"

Chang Bo fell ill, and Laozi said, "Master, your illness is severe. Can you not teach your disciples?" Chang Bo then opened his mouth wide for Laozi to see and said, "Is my tongue still there?" Laozi replied, "Yes, it is." Chang Bo asked, "Is it not soft?" Laozi replied, "Yes, it is." Chang Bo then asked, "Are my teeth gone?" Laozi replied, "Yes, they are." Chang Bo asked, "Is it not hard?" Laozi replied, "Yes, it is." Chang Bo said, "All things in the world are complete." Zichan heard a woman crying and had someone arrest her. It turned out she was the one who killed her husband. The driver asked, "How did you know?" Zichan replied, "She is the one who is close to him. When he is sick, she worries; when he dies, she mourns. But now that he is dead, she does not mourn but fears. Therefore, I knew there was something wrong." In the Sui Dynasty, Xu Deyan's wife Chen Shi was the sister of Emperor Wen of Sui. Afraid of chaos and unable to protect each other, Xu Deyan broke a mirror and divided it, so that in the future, they would know each other's fate. They agreed to meet on the fifth day of the fifth month, each holding half of the mirror and selling it in the market, in hopes of reuniting. On the appointed day, Xu Deyan went to the market and found a broken mirror. He then wrote on the back of it, "The mirror and the person went together; the mirror returns, but the person does not. There is no longer the shadow of Chang'e; only the half-moon's light remains." At that time, Chen Shi was loved by Yang Su. When she saw the mirror, she ordered Xu Deyan to drink with her. They sat in a circle, and she ordered Chen Shi to compose a poem. After the poem was written, she returned the mirror to Xu Deyan. Chen Shi's poem said, "Today, what has changed? A new official faces an old official. Neither smiling nor crying, she dared not. Only now does she realize how difficult it is to be a person." Yang Su was moved by this and returned the mirror to Xu Deyan.

In the beginning, when the universe was just starting, only Nüwa and her brother were on Mount Kunlun, and there were no people in the world. They discussed becoming husband and wife but felt ashamed. Her brother then took his sister to Mount Kunlun and said, "If heaven ordains that we, my brother and sister, should become husband and wife, the smoke will merge; if not, it will disperse." The smoke merged, and her sister came to join her brother. They then made a fan from grass to cover their faces. Today, when people take a wife, they hold a fan, symbolizing this event. Yang Jue and Zuo Botao were two virtuous friends. They traveled together and encountered a storm, running out of food. They decided they could not both survive. Yang Jue gave his food to Zuo Botao, allowing him to survive, while Yang Jue entered an empty tree and died of starvation. Liu Rengui served as a high-ranking official. His father and brothers were all local officials in Beihai County. Someone advised him, "If you are registered under the same household as your relatives, you alone will suffer from the labor duties." He responded accordingly.

Zhang Liang, courtesy name Zifang, was a young man when he walked to Yi Bridge. Suddenly, he saw an old man fall off the bridge, and the old man looked at Zhang Liang and said, "Can you fetch my shoes?" Zhang Liang wanted to beat him but, seeing that he was old, knelt down to fetch the shoes for him. The old man said, "You can be taught. Meet me tomorrow at dawn." Zhang Liang went, but the old man was already on the bridge. He told Zhang Liang, "We had an appointment, why were you late? Come back tomorrow." Zhang Liang then went at midnight, and after a short while, the old man arrived, took a book from his chest and gave it to Zhang Liang, saying, "Read this, it will make you a teacher to kings." He then sent Zhang Liang away and warned him, "In ten years, we will meet again in Jibei Gucheng, that will be me." Zhang Liang assisted King Han, becoming the emperor's teacher. Later, when he went to Jibei Gucheng to inquire, people said, "This is the Temple of the Yellow Emperor." He then established a temple there. Emperor Wu of Han exiled King Liu An of Huainan to Shu and Ba, where he died on the way. People sang, "A foot of cloth, still can be sewn; a measure of rice, still can be pounded. Brothers cannot coexist." According to *Han Feizi*: King Qi loved to listen to the yu (a type of wind instrument), and whenever he wanted to listen, he would have three hundred people play it together. Mr. Nanguo, who did not know how to play the yu, joined the group of a hundred and pretended to play it to receive俸禄 (salary). After King Xuan died, King Qi ascended the throne and said, "I love to listen to the yu, and I want each person to play it individually." Mr. Nanguo was ashamed and fled. Today, those who receive俸禄 without genuine skill are also many like this.
